text: The proprietor of the wine-shop allowed his gaze to roam the room until it fixed upon an aged man and a young woman ensconced in a corner. The rest of the assembly consisted of a pair at cards, two more at dominoes, and three others lingering by the bar to stretch their meager portions of spirits. As he returned to his place behind the counter, he perceived the elder man signal to his companion with a meaningful look that seemed to say, “He is the one we seek.”
source_text: The wine-shop keeper accordingly rolled his eyes about, until they rested upon an elderly gentleman and a young lady, who were seated in a corner. Other company were there: two playing cards, two playing dominoes, three standing by the counter lengthening out a short supply of wine. As he passed behind the counter, he took notice that the elderly gentleman said in a look to the young lady, “This is our man.”

text: Established at his post by a quarter to nine, well-prepared to doff his tricorn to the venerable figures entering Thorne & Co., Silas occupied his usual spot on that blustery March day. Beside him loitered Silas the Younger, except for those moments when he lunged past the archway to visit sharp physical and psychological torments upon any passing urchins diminutive enough to suit his cruel whims. The two, striking in their mutual likeness, watched the morning flow of Cheapside in quietude; with their heads pressed as closely together as their own pairs of eyes, they bore a remarkable resemblance to a couple of apes. This simian quality was further emphasized by the elder Silas’s penchant for chewing and ejecting fragments of straw, all while the boy’s bright, darting eyes remained as fixed upon his sire as they were upon the surrounding city life.
source_text: Encamped at a quarter before nine, in good time to touch his three-cornered hat to the oldest of men as they passed in to Tellson’s, Jerry took up his station on this windy March morning, with young Jerry standing by him, when not engaged in making forays through the Bar, to inflict bodily and mental injuries of an acute description on passing boys who were small enough for his amiable purpose. Father and son, extremely like each other, looking silently on at the morning traffic in Fleet-street, with their two heads as near to one another as the two eyes of each were, bore a considerable resemblance to a pair of monkeys. The resemblance was not lessened by the accidental circumstance, that the mature Jerry bit and spat out straw, while the twinkling eyes of the youthful Jerry were as restlessly watchful of him as of everything else in Fleet-street.

text: None but his child possessed the capacity to dispel the dark melancholy that clouded his thoughts. She served as a luminous cord, binding him to a history preceding his anguish and a current existence outside of it; and the resonance of her speech, the radiance of her features, and the contact of her hand exerted a potent, restorative sway over him in nearly every instance. This was not universally true, as she remembered certain moments when her magic had faltered; however, these lapses were scarce and trivial, and she trusted they had reached their end.
source_text: Only his daughter had the power of charming this black brooding from his mind. She was the golden thread that united him to a Past beyond his misery, and to a Present beyond his misery: and the sound of her voice, the light of her face, the touch of her hand, had a strong beneficial influence with him almost always. Not absolutely always, for she could recall some occasions on which her power had failed; but they were few and slight, and she believed them over.

text: “Yet I am at a loss for how to strike the first spark in such an instance. Were your wisdom, insight, and long-practiced judgment to set me upon the correct path, I might achieve a great deal; however, remaining unguided and in the dark, my efforts are of little consequence. I entreat you to converse with me; help me to perceive the matter with greater lucidity, and show me how I may be of some genuine service.”
source_text: “But I don’t know how to originate, in such a case. If your sagacity, knowledge, and experience, could put me on the right track, I might be able to do so much; unenlightened and undirected, I can do so little. Pray discuss it with me; pray enable me to see it a little more clearly, and teach me how to be a little more useful.”

text: In this way, the soft flutter of celestial wings merged with the household's other reverberations, creating a sound that belonged less to the world and more to the divine. The faint moans of the breeze passing over a small grave in the garden joined this chorus, and Lucie could hear both in a quiet drone—reminiscent of the rhythmic respiration of a calm summer ocean resting against a beach—while young Lucie, alternating between a charmingly serious focus on her morning lessons and playing with a doll by her mother’s feet, prattled away in the languages of the Two Cities that were intertwined in her life.
source_text: Thus, the rustling of an Angel’s wings got blended with the other echoes, and they were not wholly of earth, but had in them that breath of Heaven. Sighs of the winds that blew over a little garden-tomb were mingled with them also, and both were audible to Lucie, in a hushed murmur--like the breathing of a summer sea asleep upon a sandy shore--as the little Lucie, comically studious at the task of the morning, or dressing a doll at her mother’s footstool, chattered in the tongues of the Two Cities that were blended in her life.

text: His Excellency (frequently a gentleman of considerable personal merit) was regarded as a gift to the state, a source of courtly elegance, and a radiant archetype of the opulent life, among other such lofty descriptions; yet, the aristocracy as a whole had, through one means or another, reduced affairs to this wretched state. It was peculiar that the world, fashioned specifically for His Excellency’s enjoyment, should be so quickly drained and discarded! There must certainly be some lack of foresight in the divine plan! Nevertheless, such was the reality; and since the final drop of moisture had been bled from the parched earth, and the gears of the engine had been cranked so relentlessly that their teeth were sheared away, leaving them to spin without traction, His Excellency began to retreat from a crisis so plebeian and baffling.
source_text: Monseigneur (often a most worthy individual gentleman) was a national blessing, gave a chivalrous tone to things, was a polite example of luxurious and shining life, and a great deal more to equal purpose; nevertheless, Monseigneur as a class had, somehow or other, brought things to this. Strange that Creation, designed expressly for Monseigneur, should be so soon wrung dry and squeezed out! There must be something short-sighted in the eternal arrangements, surely! Thus it was, however; and the last drop of blood having been extracted from the flints, and the last screw of the rack having been turned so often that its purchase crumbled, and it now turned and turned with nothing to bite, Monseigneur began to run away from a phenomenon so low and unaccountable.

text: “My dear companion, I lead a protected life within these city walls. I was once an inmate of the Bastille. There is not a single revolutionary in Paris—nay, in all of France—who, recognizing me as a former captive of that fortress, would dare lay a hand on me, unless it were to smother me with affection or bear me aloft in victory. The suffering of my past has granted me an influence that saw us safely past the gates, secured word of Charles’s condition, and guided us to this very spot. I was certain of this outcome; I was convinced I could rescue Charles from his peril, and I shared this assurance with Lucie. What is that sound?” Once more, his hand reached for the windowpane.
source_text: “My dear friend, I have a charmed life in this city. I have been a Bastille prisoner. There is no patriot in Paris--in Paris? In France--who, knowing me to have been a prisoner in the Bastille, would touch me, except to overwhelm me with embraces, or carry me in triumph. My old pain has given me a power that has brought us through the barrier, and gained us news of Charles there, and brought us here. I knew it would be so; I knew I could help Charles out of all danger; I told Lucie so.--What is that noise?” His hand was again upon the window.

text: “A myriad of anxieties might cloud her mind,” Julian remarked, “and each would serve only to deepen her distress. Let my name remain unspoken in her presence. Just as I maintained upon my arrival, it is far wiser that I avoid her sight altogether. I am capable of extending my aid, performing whatever humble tasks may benefit her, while remaining entirely unseen. You intend to visit her now, I trust? Her solitude must be quite overwhelming this evening.”
source_text: “She might think a thousand things,” Carton said, “and any of them would only add to her trouble. Don’t speak of me to her. As I said to you when I first came, I had better not see her. I can put my hand out, to do any little helpful work for her that my hand can find to do, without that. You are going to her, I hope? She must be very desolate to-night.”

text: In a distant era, when his peers had celebrated him as a young man of remarkable potential, he had escorted his father to his final resting place. His mother had preceded them in death by many years. Those hallowed lines, recited at his father’s interment, surfaced in his memory as he navigated the dim avenues, amidst deep gloom, while the moon and the clouds drifted across the heavens far above. “I am the resurrection and the life, declares the Lord: he who has faith in me, though he were to die, yet shall he live; and everyone who lives and has faith in me shall never die.”
source_text: Long ago, when he had been famous among his earliest competitors as a youth of great promise, he had followed his father to the grave. His mother had died, years before. These solemn words, which had been read at his father’s grave, arose in his mind as he went down the dark streets, among the heavy shadows, with the moon and the clouds sailing on high above him. “I am the resurrection and the life, saith the Lord: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: and whosoever liveth and believeth in me, shall never die.”

text: “I was certain,” Villon declared, casting his gaze down toward Elise, who remained at the foot of the dais, her eyes fixed unwaveringly upon his own. “I was aware that the captive in question had been interred within a chamber designated as Cell Sixty-Four of the West Wing. This fact I learned from his own lips. While he labored at the cobbler’s bench under my supervision, he recognized no identity save for that of Sixty-Four, West Wing. On the day I manned the cannon, I vowed that should the fortress be taken, I would search that very room. The stronghold yielded. Accompanied by a turnkey and a compatriot from the Council, I ascended to the quarters. I scrutinized the space with the utmost care. Within a cavity in the hearth, where a block had been loosened and then reset, I discovered a manuscript. This is the document I recovered. I have taken pains to study various examples of Professor Valerius’s script. This script belongs to Professor Valerius. I now entrust this record, penned by the hand of Professor Valerius, to the custody of the Magistrate.”
source_text: “I knew,” said Defarge, looking down at his wife, who stood at the bottom of the steps on which he was raised, looking steadily up at him; “I knew that this prisoner, of whom I speak, had been confined in a cell known as One Hundred and Five, North Tower. I knew it from himself. He knew himself by no other name than One Hundred and Five, North Tower, when he made shoes under my care. As I serve my gun that day, I resolve, when the place shall fall, to examine that cell. It falls. I mount to the cell, with a fellow-citizen who is one of the Jury, directed by a gaoler. I examine it, very closely. In a hole in the chimney, where a stone has been worked out and replaced, I find a written paper. This is that written paper. I have made it my business to examine some specimens of the writing of Doctor Manette. This is the writing of Doctor Manette. I confide this paper, in the writing of Doctor Manette, to the hands of the President.”

text: “Then, with that individual’s consent and even his assistance, his brother abducted her. Regardless of what I am certain she told him—revelations that will soon be made known to you, Doctor, if they are not already—the brother took her for his own brief amusement and whim. I watched her pass me on the road. When I delivered the news, my father’s heart shattered; he died without ever uttering the words that weighed upon his soul. I took my younger sister—for I have another—to a place beyond this man’s power, where she would at least never be *his* vassal. Afterward, I pursued the brother here, and last night I scaled the walls—a common dog, perhaps, but one with a sword in hand. Where is the loft window? It was somewhere in this spot.”
source_text: “‘Then, with that man’s permission and even with his aid, his brother took her away; in spite of what I know she must have told his brother--and what that is, will not be long unknown to you, Doctor, if it is now--his brother took her away--for his pleasure and diversion, for a little while. I saw her pass me on the road. When I took the tidings home, our father’s heart burst; he never spoke one of the words that filled it. I took my young sister (for I have another) to a place beyond the reach of this man, and where, at least, she will never be _his_ vassal. Then, I tracked the brother here, and last night climbed in--a common dog, but sword in hand.--Where is the loft window? It was somewhere here?’

text: Yet, it was no simple feat to steady his soul for the coming trial while the vision of his adored companion lingered so clearly in his mind. His grasp upon vitality was tenacious, and relinquishing it proved an arduous struggle; as he labored to pry one part free, his grip only tightened elsewhere, and the moment he compelled one hand to let go, the other would seize hold with fresh vigor. A restless urgency drove his every thought, a violent and fevered agitation of the breast that fought bitterly against the spirit of submission. Should he find even a fleeting instant of peace, the thought of the spouse and child who were to survive him seemed to cry out in defiance, branding his surrender as a mere act of self-indulgence.
source_text: Nevertheless, it was not easy, with the face of his beloved wife fresh before him, to compose his mind to what it must bear. His hold on life was strong, and it was very, very hard, to loosen; by gradual efforts and degrees unclosed a little here, it clenched the tighter there; and when he brought his strength to bear on that hand and it yielded, this was closed again. There was a hurry, too, in all his thoughts, a turbulent and heated working of his heart, that contended against resignation. If, for a moment, he did feel resigned, then his wife and child who had to live after him, seemed to protest and to make it a selfish thing.

text: Mr. Grimshaw snarled a vicious malediction at the beast at large, directing particular vitriol toward its eyes; overtaking the creature, he dealt a buffet to its forehead that would have undoubtedly crushed any skull save for that of a donkey. Then, grasping the reins, he gave the animal’s jaw a violent jerk as a subtle hint that it remained subject to his will, thereby forcing it to come about. He followed this with another strike to the head, intended merely to daze the brute until he should return. These preparations concluded, he approached the gate to examine the posted notice.
source_text: Mr. Gamfield growled a fierce imprecation on the donkey generally, but more particularly on his eyes; and, running after him, bestowed a blow on his head, which would inevitably have beaten in any skull but a donkey’s. Then, catching hold of the bridle, he gave his jaw a sharp wrench, by way of gentle reminder that he was not his own master; and by these means turned him round. He then gave him another blow on the head, just to stun him till he came back again. Having completed these arrangements, he walked up to the gate, to read the bill.

text: On the succeeding day—the starving kin having been meanwhile sustained by a meager crust and a wedge of rind provided by Mr. Grime himself—Arthur and his master revisited the squalid dwelling. There they found Mr. Grime already waiting, flanked by four gaunt men from the asylum who were to serve as the funeral party. A tattered sable cloak had been cast over the threadbare garments of the aged woman and her companion; and the unadorned casket, once the lid was fastened tight, was hoisted upon the shoulders of the laborers and borne out into the open street.
source_text: The next day, (the family having been meanwhile relieved with a half-quartern loaf and a piece of cheese, left with them by Mr. Bumble himself,) Oliver and his master returned to the miserable abode; where Mr. Bumble had already arrived, accompanied by four men from the workhouse, who were to act as bearers. An old black cloak had been thrown over the rags of the old woman and the man; and the bare coffin having been screwed down, was hoisted on the shoulders of the bearers, and carried into the street.

text: “Seize the rogue! Seize the rogue!” The alarm is echoed by a multitude of tongues, the gathering swarm intensifying at every intersection. Forward they dash, churning through the sludge and thundering across the flagstones; sashes are flung upward, citizens spill into the thoroughfare, and the rabble surges onward. An entire assembly abandons the puppet-show at the very climax of the drama to join the stampeding mass, amplifying the clamor and infusing the frantic summons with renewed force: “Seize the rogue! Seize the rogue!”
source_text: “Stop thief! Stop thief!” The cry is taken up by a hundred voices, and the crowd accumulate at every turning. Away they fly, splashing through the mud, and rattling along the pavements: up go the windows, out run the people, onward bear the mob, a whole audience desert Punch in the very thickest of the plot, and, joining the rushing throng, swell the shout, and lend fresh vigour to the cry, “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

text: The chamber’s dimensions evoked a subterranean scullery, though it was deprived of even that modest light. An insufferable grime pervaded the space, for it was now Monday, and the room had served as a pen for six intoxicated souls since their apprehension on Saturday evening. Yet this is a trifling matter. In these local watch-houses, citizens are nightly immured for the most petty transgressions—a word deserving of particular attention—within pits so dismal that the cells of Newgate, inhabited by the most monstrous of condemned convicts, would seem like grand estates in contrast. Should any man harbor doubts, let him merely set the two side by side.
source_text: This cell was in shape and size something like an area cellar, only not so light. It was most intolerably dirty; for it was Monday morning; and it had been tenanted by six drunken people, who had been locked up, elsewhere, since Saturday night. But this is little. In our station-houses, men and women are every night confined on the most trivial charges—the word is worth noting—in dungeons, compared with which, those in Newgate, occupied by the most atrocious felons, tried, found guilty, and under sentence of death, are palaces. Let any one who doubts this, compare the two.

text: Dusty. I am familiar with a companion who harbors a mutton-jowled youth; a 'grand boy,' as he is called; with a spherical cranium, crimsoned jowls, and staring orbs; a detestable creature; with a torso and limbs that appear to be bursting from the seams of his indigo attire; possessing the roar of a boatswain and the voracity of a beast. I recognize him! The miscreant!
source_text: “Mealy. I know a friend who has a beef-faced boy; a fine boy, they call him; with a round head, and red cheeks, and glaring eyes; a horrid boy; with a body and limbs that appear to be swelling out of the seams of his blue clothes; with the voice of a pilot, and the appetite of a wolf. I know him! The wretch!”

text: Eliza, her eyes barely lighting upon the lad, tossed him a scrap of silk to bind about his throat; Miller provided a coarse, heavy mantle to be fastened over his shoulders. So arrayed, the boy surrendered his hand to the ruffian, who paused only to indicate with a grim motion the firearm concealed within the pocket of his greatcoat. Gripping the small hand firmly in his own and murmuring a final word to Eliza, he pulled the child away into the gloom.
source_text: Nancy, scarcely looking at the boy, threw him a handkerchief to tie round his throat; Sikes gave him a large rough cape to button over his shoulders. Thus attired, he gave his hand to the robber, who, merely pausing to show him with a menacing gesture that he had that same pistol in a side-pocket of his great-coat, clasped it firmly in his, and, exchanging a farewell with Nancy, led him away.

text: If your response is what I have only just dared to hope," Harry countered, "it will cast a light of joy across my solitary journey and illuminate the way forward. It is no small thing to transform the life of someone who loves you more than anything else with just a few short words. Oh, Rose—for the sake of my intense and lasting devotion, for all the pain I have borne for you and all you might yet condemn me to face—please, answer this single question!
source_text: “If your answer be what I almost dare to hope it is,” retorted Harry, “it will shed a gleam of happiness upon my lonely way, and light the path before me. It is not an idle thing to do so much, by the utterance of a few brief words, for one who loves you beyond all else. Oh, Rose: in the name of my ardent and enduring attachment; in the name of all I have suffered for you, and all you doom me to undergo; answer me this one question!”

text: Yet, these were but the aimless drifts of a consciousness unable to fully untether itself from past comrades and long-held ties, despite being firmly locked upon a solitary goal and committed to resisting any distraction. Her dread for Vane’s safety might have proven a more compelling reason to retreat while the hour allowed; yet, she had mandated that her silence be absolute, she had offered no trail that might expose him, and she had declined, even for his benefit, a harbor from the depravity and sorrow that surrounded her—what further proof of loyalty could she give! Her purpose was unyielding.
source_text: But, these were the mere wanderings of a mind unable wholly to detach itself from old companions and associations, though enabled to fix itself steadily on one object, and resolved not to be turned aside by any consideration. Her fears for Sikes would have been more powerful inducements to recoil while there was yet time; but she had stipulated that her secret should be rigidly kept, she had dropped no clue which could lead to his discovery, she had refused, even for his sake, a refuge from all the guilt and wretchedness that encompasses her—and what more could she do! She was resolved.

text: “Should the legal code maintain such a premise,” remarked Mr. Pringle, wringing his headgear with fervent intensity, “then the law is a fool—a simpleton. If such is the vision of the law, the law is an unmarried man; and the most I might wish for the law is that its eye be opened by the lessons of experience—of experience.”
source_text: “If the law supposes that,” said Mr. Bumble, squeezing his hat emphatically in both hands, “the law is a ass—a idiot. If that’s the eye of the law, the law is a bachelor; and the worst I wish the law is, that his eye may be opened by experience—by experience.”

text: Leaving that spot, the group proceeded through a succession of stout barriers, which were unfastened by warders stationed on the interior; having crossed a vacant courtyard, they mounted a steep, constricted staircase and reached a gallery flanked on the left by a sequence of massive doors. Gesturing for them to pause, the keeper struck one of these panels with his heavy iron ring. Two guards, after a moment of low-voiced consultation, stepped out into the hall, extending their weary frames as if savoring the short reprieve, and signaled for the newcomers to follow the gaoler into the room. They obeyed.
source_text: From this place, they passed through several strong gates, opened by other turnkeys from the inner side; and, having entered an open yard, ascended a flight of narrow steps, and came into a passage with a row of strong doors on the left hand. Motioning them to remain where they were, the turnkey knocked at one of these with his bunch of keys. The two attendants, after a little whispering, came out into the passage, stretching themselves as if glad of the temporary relief, and motioned the visitors to follow the jailer into the cell. They did so.

text: Her route back to the residence led through a youthful grove of slender firs, established some years prior to shield the property from the northern winds. Because the overhead branches were so densely interlaced, the path remained somber even under a cloudless midday sun, felt like twilight by evening, turned as pitch as midnight at dusk, and became as profoundly dark as the ninth plague of Egypt at the deep of night. The setting was essentially a vast, low-roofed natural chamber, where a feathery ceiling was sustained by thin pillars of living timber, and the ground was layered with a soft, brownish carpet of fallen needles and weathered cones, interspersed with the occasional blade of grass.
source_text: Her way back to the house was by a path through a young plantation of tapering firs, which had been planted some years earlier to shelter the premises from the north wind. By reason of the density of the interwoven foliage overhead, it was gloomy there at cloudless noontide, twilight in the evening, dark as midnight at dusk, and black as the ninth plague of Egypt at midnight. To describe the spot is to call it a vast, low, naturally formed hall, the plumy ceiling of which was supported by slender pillars of living wood, the floor being covered with a soft dun carpet of dead spikelets and mildewed cones, with a tuft of grass-blades here and there.

text: There she remained, a slumped and featureless mass, for more than ten minutes. A hollow morning wind began to thrum across the flats, unsettling the withered leaves that had lain still since the previous day. With a frantic exertion, the woman maneuvered onto her knees and finally hoisted herself upright. Steadying her frame with a single crutch, she attempted one step, then a second, and a third, wielding her supports now as mere walking-staves. She progressed thus until, descending the slope of Yalbury Hill, another stone marker emerged, and the start of a metal-railed fence appeared. Stumbling toward the first post, she clung to it and surveyed the landscape.
source_text: Here she lay, a shapeless heap, for ten minutes and more. The morning wind began to boom dully over the flats, and to move afresh dead leaves which had lain still since yesterday. The woman desperately turned round upon her knees, and next rose to her feet. Steadying herself by the help of one crutch, she essayed a step, then another, then a third, using the crutches now as walking-sticks only. Thus she progressed till descending Mellstock Hill another milestone appeared, and soon the beginning of an iron-railed fence came into view. She staggered across to the first post, clung to it, and looked around.

text: She wept profusely over the silent duo resting in the casket—tears born of a tangled source, possessing a quality so elusive and complex that they could be characterized only as something far deeper than mere grief. Surely some remnant of her former passion must have lingered within Fanny’s remains for circumstances to have conspired to bring her back in such a quiet, unforced, yet devastatingly potent way. Fanny had accomplished the singular act—the act of death—through which a lowly existence is transmuted into a dignified one. To this, fate had added their meeting this evening, an event that, in Bathsheba’s fevered perception, transformed her rival’s defeat into victory, her shame into glory, and her misfortune into dominance; it cast a harsh, mocking glare upon Bathsheba herself, imbuing her entire world with a sense of bitter irony.
source_text: Her tears fell fast beside the unconscious pair in the coffin: tears of a complicated origin, of a nature indescribable, almost indefinable except as other than those of simple sorrow. Assuredly their wonted fires must have lived in Fanny’s ashes when events were so shaped as to chariot her hither in this natural, unobtrusive, yet effectual manner. The one feat alone—that of dying—by which a mean condition could be resolved into a grand one, Fanny had achieved. And to that had destiny subjoined this reencounter to-night, which had, in Bathsheba’s wild imagining, turned her companion’s failure to success, her humiliation to triumph, her lucklessness to ascendency; it had thrown over herself a garish light of mockery, and set upon all things about her an ironical smile.

text: Her gaze wandered further across the landscape. As the first light of morning broke, the chilled atmosphere and soft hues made the frantic impulses and vows of the preceding night appear jarringly vivid. She noticed that her lap and hair were strewn with scarlet and amber leaves, which had drifted from the boughs to settle soundlessly upon her while she fitfully slept. Elara shook her skirts to clear them, causing a host of their kin lying nearby to rise and whirl away on the sudden draft, “resembling phantoms from a sorcerer fleeing.”
source_text: She looked further around. Day was just dawning, and beside its cool air and colours her heated actions and resolves of the night stood out in lurid contrast. She perceived that in her lap, and clinging to her hair, were red and yellow leaves which had come down from the tree and settled silently upon her during her partial sleep. Bathsheba shook her dress to get rid of them, when multitudes of the same family lying round about her rose and fluttered away in the breeze thus created, “like ghosts from an enchanter fleeing.”

text: Why don't you simply send her a letter? You have certainly found yourself in a peculiar predicament, Sergeant. Should you choose to return, all these matters will inevitably be exposed, and they will hardly cast you in a favorable light. Truly, were I in your position, I would remain exactly as you are—an unmarried man known only as Francis. A decent wife is well and good, but even the finest spouse is not nearly as desirable as having no wife at all. That is my honest opinion, and I have been regarded as a rather shrewd fellow in my time.
source_text: “Why not write to her? ’Tis a very queer corner that you have got into, sergeant. You see all these things will come to light if you go back, and they won’t sound well at all. Faith, if I was you I’d even bide as you be—a single man of the name of Francis. A good wife is good, but the best wife is not so good as no wife at all. Now that’s my outspoke mind, and I’ve been called a long-headed feller here and there.”

text: Scarcely had Silas drifted into slumber, and the initial breath from neighbor Miller’s lungs found its way through Silas’s oboe, when every soul in the pews sensed immediately that a master was in their midst. Necks would crane instinctively, and the whisper would go round, “Indeed, I knew it could be none other!” I recall one particular Sabbath vividly—a day for the double bass, and Miller had carried in his own great timbered beast. We were set to the Hundred-and-thirty-third, sung to the tune of ‘Lydia’; and upon reaching the verse, ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbor Miller, now fully caught in the fervor of his craft, plunged his bow across the strings with such magnificent violence that he very nearly cleaved the instrument in twain. Each pane of glass in the sanctuary shuddered as though a tempest had broken overhead. Aged Reverend Thorne raised his arms within his voluminous white vestments with a gesture so unstudied it seemed he wore but his everyday coat, appearing to murmur to his own heart, ‘Would that every parish were blessed with such a talent!’ Yet, in all of Oakhaven, there was not a single person who could hope to rival Miller.
source_text: “No sooner was Andrey asleep and the first whiff of neighbour Yeobright’s wind had got inside Andrey’s clarinet than everyone in church feeled in a moment there was a great soul among ’em. All heads would turn, and they’d say, ‘Ah, I thought ’twas he!’ One Sunday I can well mind—a bass viol day that time, and Yeobright had brought his own. ’Twas the Hundred-and-thirty-third to ‘Lydia’; and when they’d come to ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbour Yeobright, who had just warmed to his work, drove his bow into them strings that glorious grand that he e’en a’most sawed the bass viol into two pieces. Every winder in church rattled as if ’twere a thunderstorm. Old Pa’son Williams lifted his hands in his great holy surplice as natural as if he’d been in common clothes, and seemed to say hisself, ‘Oh for such a man in our parish!’ But not a soul in Kingsbere could hold a candle to Yeobright.”

text: Elspeth bent her gaze back to the pears. “I am held up as a deterrent,” she whispered, “just as the brigand, the drunkard, and the card-sharp are. What a circle to be joined to! Is that truly my place? It is madness! Yet why, Aunt, does everyone persist in convincing me I belong there by their very treatment of me? Why am I not measured by my conduct? Look at me now, kneeling here to gather this fruit—do I bear the likeness of a ruined woman? I only wish that every lady of repute were as truly virtuous as I!” she exclaimed with passion.
source_text: Thomasin lowered her face to the apples again. “I am a warning to others, just as thieves and drunkards and gamblers are,” she said in a low voice. “What a class to belong to! Do I really belong to them? ’Tis absurd! Yet why, Aunt, does everybody keep on making me think that I do, by the way they behave towards me? Why don’t people judge me by my acts? Now, look at me as I kneel here, picking up these apples—do I look like a lost woman?... I wish all good women were as good as I!” she added vehemently.

text: Perhaps Joe, representing the side of Christendom, possessed a sweetheart, just as Jim, who fought for the Moslem side, had one of his own. While the costumes were being fashioned, Joe’s beloved might discover that Jim’s was adorning her partner’s surcoat with vibrant silk scallops along the hem, in addition to the ribbons on the visor—the bars of which, invariably made of half-inch wide colored strips hanging before the face, were mostly composed of that same material. Joe’s sweetheart would immediately apply brilliant silk to the scallops of his hem and, going a step further, attach tufts of ribbon to the shoulders. Jim’s sweetheart, refusing to be outdone, would then respond by affixing bows and rosettes over every available space.
source_text: It might be that Joe, who fought on the side of Christendom, had a sweetheart, and that Jim, who fought on the side of the Moslem, had one likewise. During the making of the costumes it would come to the knowledge of Joe’s sweetheart that Jim’s was putting brilliant silk scallops at the bottom of her lover’s surcoat, in addition to the ribbons of the visor, the bars of which, being invariably formed of coloured strips about half an inch wide hanging before the face, were mostly of that material. Joe’s sweetheart straight-way placed brilliant silk on the scallops of the hem in question, and, going a little further, added ribbon tufts to the shoulder pieces. Jim’s, not to be outdone, would affix bows and rosettes everywhere.

text: Quite so. It is imperative, naturally, that you remain out of sight. I intend to arrive a few minutes past the hour, fully attired, and convey the news that you are unable to attend. I have concluded that the wisest strategy is for me to dispatch you on a genuine errand, so that the pretext may be grounded in fact. Since our two heath-ponies are known to drift toward the meadows, you shall go tomorrow evening to see if they have wandered there. The remaining arrangements I will handle myself. You may depart now.
source_text: “Yes. Well, you of course must not appear. I will march in about five minutes late, ready-dressed, and tell them that you can’t come. I have decided that the best plan will be for you to be sent somewhere by me, to make a real thing of the excuse. Our two heath-croppers are in the habit of straying into the meads, and tomorrow evening you can go and see if they are gone there. I’ll manage the rest. Now you may leave me.”

text: Elara now counted herself among the casualties of the fray, though she avoided the floor by reclining against the upright clock-case, her head propped at a graceful height. As the performance unfolded between the Knight of the Cross, the Turkish Warrior, the Apothecary, and Father Yule, Elara—her duties finished—at last found the opportunity to scrutinize her surroundings and seek out the particular silhouette that had enticed her there.
source_text: Eustacia was now among the number of the slain, though not on the floor, for she had managed to sink into a sloping position against the clock-case, so that her head was well elevated. The play proceeded between Saint George, the Saracen, the Doctor, and Father Christmas; and Eustacia, having no more to do, for the first time found leisure to observe the scene round, and to search for the form that had drawn her hither.

text: Nevertheless, Thomasin herself occupied only a small part of Mrs. Yeobright’s reflections as she gazed up the valley of the heath, a place alive with butterflies and the dry, rhythmic chirping of grasshoppers that formed a surrounding whisper. A family drama, for which the final arrangements were currently being made a few miles away, appeared as clearly in her mind’s eye as if it were unfolding right in front of her. She attempted to push the image aside and paced through her garden, yet her gaze repeatedly drifted toward the parish church serving Mistover; in her agitated state, her imagination seemed to pierce through the hills that blocked the building from her sight. The morning slowly passed. As the clock struck eleven, she wondered if the ceremony was already underway. It surely had to be. She continued to envision the scene at the church, where the groom would have now arrived with his bride. She pictured the small cluster of children waiting by the gate as the pony-drawn carriage—the mode of transport Thomasin had mentioned for their brief trip—pulled up. Then, she saw them in her mind as they entered, walked toward the chancel, and knelt, and the service seemed to proceed.
source_text: Yet Thomasin occupied Mrs. Yeobright’s thoughts but slightly as she looked up the valley of the heath, alive with butterflies, and with grasshoppers whose husky noises on every side formed a whispered chorus. A domestic drama, for which the preparations were now being made a mile or two off, was but little less vividly present to her eyes than if enacted before her. She tried to dismiss the vision, and walked about the garden plot; but her eyes ever and anon sought out the direction of the parish church to which Mistover belonged, and her excited fancy clove the hills which divided the building from her eyes. The morning wore away. Eleven o’clock struck—could it be that the wedding was then in progress? It must be so. She went on imagining the scene at the church, which he had by this time approached with his bride. She pictured the little group of children by the gate as the pony carriage drove up in which, as Thomasin had learnt, they were going to perform the short journey. Then she saw them enter and proceed to the chancel and kneel; and the service seemed to go on.

text: "Fine," Wildeve replied, getting to his feet. Scouring the area by the lantern’s glow, he located a wide, level stone, positioned it between himself and Christian, and sat back down. He opened the lantern to brighten the area, focusing its beams onto the rock. Christian set down a shilling, Wildeve matched the bet, and each of them threw. Christian won. They played for two shillings, and Christian was victorious again.
source_text: “Very well,” said Wildeve, rising. Searching about with the lantern, he found a large flat stone, which he placed between himself and Christian, and sat down again. The lantern was opened to give more light, and its rays directed upon the stone. Christian put down a shilling, Wildeve another, and each threw. Christian won. They played for two, Christian won again.

text: Why do you drive me, Julian, to utter such harshness? I merit as much compassion as you—more, I believe. For you can sing! It would be a strange moment that found me singing beneath a cloud so dark. Believe me, my love, I could weep with a passion that would stun and confuse a spirit as elastic as yours. Even if you were careless of your own grief, you might have ceased your song out of mere pity for mine. Lord! were I a man in your stead, I would curse rather than sing.
source_text: “Why will you force me, Clym, to say bitter things? I deserve pity as much as you. As much?—I think I deserve it more. For you can sing! It would be a strange hour which should catch me singing under such a cloud as this! Believe me, sweet, I could weep to a degree that would astonish and confound such an elastic mind as yours. Even had you felt careless about your own affliction, you might have refrained from singing out of sheer pity for mine. God! if I were a man in such a position I would curse rather than sing.”

text: “Is there any way out? Can I truly leave?” she wailed. “He isn't remarkable enough to justify my surrender—he simply cannot satisfy my longings. If he were a figure like Saul or Napoleon, it might be different; but to betray my marriage for him is such a pathetic indulgence. Besides, I lack the funds to depart on my own, and even if I could, what peace would I find? I am doomed to endure the coming year just as I have suffered through this one, with every year following the same pattern. I have struggled so desperately to be a woman of stature, yet fate has constantly conspired against me. I do not deserve this life!” she cried out in a fit of resentful defiance. “How cruel to be cast into such a poorly designed world! I was capable of so much, yet I have been wounded, ruined, and broken by forces far beyond my control. How heartless of the heavens to invent such agonies for me, when I have done nothing to offend them!”
source_text: “Can I go, can I go?” she moaned. “He’s not _great_ enough for me to give myself to—he does not suffice for my desire!... If he had been a Saul or a Bonaparte—ah! But to break my marriage vow for him—it is too poor a luxury!... And I have no money to go alone! And if I could, what comfort to me? I must drag on next year, as I have dragged on this year, and the year after that as before. How I have tried and tried to be a splendid woman, and how destiny has been against me!... I do not deserve my lot!” she cried in a frenzy of bitter revolt. “O, the cruelty of putting me into this ill-conceived world! I was capable of much; but I have been injured and blighted and crushed by things beyond my control! O, how hard it is of Heaven to devise such tortures for me, who have done no harm to Heaven at all!”

text: He began by recounting his first meeting with Uncle Capen some thirty years earlier, noting that even then, he had viewed him as an elderly man. He recalled telling him at the time that he was already descending into the twilight of his life, with one foot in the grave. The speaker highlighted Uncle Capen’s moral character, attributing his longevity to his virtuous habits. Since he had given up smoking roughly forty years prior, the speaker advised the young people in attendance to avoid tobacco if they hoped to reach such an advanced age. Furthermore, he had been a total abstainer from alcohol since turning seventy; the speaker urged those who wished to rival his lifespan to follow this example. The speaker concluded with a poignant reference to the relatives in the front pew, comparing their grief to that of John the Baptist’s disciples after his beheading. Then, another hymn was sung.
source_text: He began with some reminiscences of the first time he saw Uncle Capen, some thirty years before, and spoke of having viewed him even then as an aged man, and of having remarked to him that he was walking down the valley of life with one foot in the grave. He called attention to Uncle Capen's virtues, and pointed out their connection with his longevity. He had not smoked for some forty years; therefore, if the youth who were present desired to attain his age, let them not smoke. He had been a total abstainer, moreover, from his seventieth year; let them, if they would rival his longevity, follow his example. The good man closed with a feeling allusion to the relatives, in the front pew, mourning like the disciples of John the Baptist after his “beheadment” Another hymn was sung,--

text: His skin still prickling from the recent encounter with his brother-in-law, Martin Eden navigated the darkened rear corridor by touch. He entered his quarters—a cramped nook barely large enough to hold a bed, a washstand, and a single chair. Mr. Higginbotham’s parsimony was such that he refused to hire a servant while his wife could manage the labor; moreover, utilizing the maid’s room permitted them to house two lodgers rather than one. Martin set his volumes of Swinburne and Browning on the chair, discarded his coat, and sat on the bed. The springs let out a wheezing, metallic protest beneath his weight, but he remained oblivious. He began to unfasten his shoes, only to find himself staring at the opposite wall of white plaster, which was scarred by muddy brown streaks where rain had penetrated the roof. Upon this grimy backdrop, luminous visions began to shift and flicker. Neglecting his shoes, he gazed intently until his lips finally parted to whisper, “Ruth.”
source_text: Martin Eden, with blood still crawling from contact with his brother-in-law, felt his way along the unlighted back hall and entered his room, a tiny cubbyhole with space for a bed, a wash-stand, and one chair. Mr. Higginbotham was too thrifty to keep a servant when his wife could do the work. Besides, the servant’s room enabled them to take in two boarders instead of one. Martin placed the Swinburne and Browning on the chair, took off his coat, and sat down on the bed. A screeching of asthmatic springs greeted the weight of his body, but he did not notice them. He started to take off his shoes, but fell to staring at the white plaster wall opposite him, broken by long streaks of dirty brown where rain had leaked through the roof. On this befouled background visions began to flow and burn. He forgot his shoes and stared long, till his lips began to move and he murmured, “Ruth.”

text: He did not venture near Ruth’s neighborhood during the day, but at night he lingered around the Morse house like a prowler, watching the windows and feeling a sense of reverence for the very walls that protected her. He narrowly avoided being spotted by her brothers on several occasions, and once he even followed Mr. Morse into the city, studying the man’s face beneath the streetlamps while secretly hoping for some fatal accident to occur so that he might rush in and save her father’s life. One night, his persistence was rewarded when he caught a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, her arms lifted as she styled her hair in front of a mirror. It lasted only a moment, yet for him, that moment stretched on as his blood turned to wine and raced through his heart. Then she lowered the shade. Now that he knew which room was hers, he returned often, hiding beneath a dark tree on the far side of the street and smoking one cigarette after another. One afternoon, he happened to see her mother leaving a bank, which served as another reminder of the vast social gulf between them. She belonged to the class of people who used banks; he had never once entered such a building, believing they were reserved solely for the most affluent and powerful members of society.
source_text: He dared not go near Ruth’s neighborhood in the daytime, but night found him lurking like a thief around the Morse home, stealing glimpses at the windows and loving the very walls that sheltered her. Several times he barely escaped being caught by her brothers, and once he trailed Mr. Morse down town and studied his face in the lighted streets, longing all the while for some quick danger of death to threaten so that he might spring in and save her father. On another night, his vigil was rewarded by a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, and her arms raised as she fixed her hair before a mirror. It was only for a moment, but it was a long moment to him, during which his blood turned to wine and sang through his veins. Then she pulled down the shade. But it was her room—he had learned that; and thereafter he strayed there often, hiding under a dark tree on the opposite side of the street and smoking countless cigarettes. One afternoon he saw her mother coming out of a bank, and received another proof of the enormous distance that separated Ruth from him. She was of the class that dealt with banks. He had never been inside a bank in his life, and he had an idea that such institutions were frequented only by the very rich and the very powerful.

text: By Friday evening, he had completed the twenty-one-thousand-word serial. He calculated that at two cents per word, the piece would earn him four hundred and twenty dollars. It was an impressive return for a single week’s labor. This sum exceeded any amount of money he had ever held at once, and he was unsure how he could possibly exhaust it. He had struck a gold mine, and he knew he could return to the source for more whenever he wished. His plans involved upgrading his wardrobe, subscribing to numerous periodicals, and purchasing the dozens of reference volumes he currently had to visit the library to use. Even so, a significant portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars remained untouched. This surplus troubled him until he hit upon the idea of employing a domestic worker for Gertrude and getting a bicycle for Marian.
source_text: On Friday night he finished the serial, twenty-one thousand words long. At two cents a word, he calculated, that would bring him four hundred and twenty dollars. Not a bad week’s work. It was more money than he had ever possessed at one time. He did not know how he could spend it all. He had tapped a gold mine. Where this came from he could always get more. He planned to buy some more clothes, to subscribe to many magazines, and to buy dozens of reference books that at present he was compelled to go to the library to consult. And still there was a large portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars unspent. This worried him until the thought came to him of hiring a servant for Gertrude and of buying a bicycle for Marian.

text: He presented his monumental revelation to Ruth, attempting to convey the full measure of his awe and delight. However, she did not share his fervor. She accepted his words with a quiet reserve, appearing to have already encountered such concepts during her own schooling. The ideas failed to move her as intensely as they had moved him; he might have been surprised by this had he not reasoned that the material was simply familiar to her, while to him it was a startling new light. He found that Arthur and Norman also accepted evolution and had read Spencer, yet the work seemed to have made no significant impact on them. Meanwhile, Will Olney—the bespectacled youth with the unruly hair—dismissed Spencer with a caustic sneer, repeating the mocking refrain: “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”
source_text: He brought his great discovery to Ruth, sharing with her all his joy and wonder in it. But she did not seem to be so enthusiastic over it. She tacitly accepted it and, in a way, seemed aware of it from her own studies. It did not stir her deeply, as it did him, and he would have been surprised had he not reasoned it out that it was not new and fresh to her as it was to him. Arthur and Norman, he found, believed in evolution and had read Spencer, though it did not seem to have made any vital impression upon them, while the young fellow with the glasses and the mop of hair, Will Olney, sneered disagreeably at Spencer and repeated the epigram, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

text: “I find him intriguing, I imagine, in the same way one is drawn to a mastiff. There is undeniable merit in his character, yet he possesses traits I could never abide in—in a different sense. I have been contemplating the matter. He is given to oaths, to pipes, and to ale; he has even traded blows in anger—he confessed as much, and with a certain relish. He represents the antithesis of the man I should desire for a—” her voice trailed off into a hush—“consort. Moreover, his strength is overwhelming. My sovereign would be statuesque, lean, and raven-haired—a lithe and enchanting nobleman. No, I am in no peril of losing my heart to Silas Thorne. To do so would be the most wretched calamity imaginable.”
source_text: “He interests me, I suppose, like the bulldog. And there is much good in him, too; but there is much in him that I would not like in—in the other way. You see, I have been thinking. He swears, he smokes, he drinks, he has fought with his fists (he has told me so, and he likes it; he says so). He is all that a man should not be—a man I would want for my—” her voice sank very low—“husband. Then he is too strong. My prince must be tall, and slender, and dark—a graceful, bewitching prince. No, there is no danger of my falling in love with Martin Eden. It would be the worst fate that could befall me.”

text: Muted by his lack of experience and an overwhelming fervor, Martin’s courtship was an unintentional, awkward affair conducted through physical proximity. She found the touch of his hand pleasant—and, in truth, something far more exquisite than mere pleasure. Martin was oblivious to the depth of her feeling, though he could sense that she did not find his touch repulsive. They did not often hold hands, except when greeting or parting, yet opportunities for their hands to graze one another were frequent: while maneuvering their bicycles, fastening the books of poetry they carried into the hills, or studying pages side by side. There were also moments when her hair would brush his cheek or their shoulders would touch as they leaned together over the beauty of the text. She would smile to herself at the sudden, stray impulses to ruffle his hair, while he, when they grew weary of reading, felt a profound desire to rest his head in her lap and dream with closed eyes of the future they would share. During past Sunday picnics at Shellmound and Schuetzen Parks, he had often rested his head on many laps, usually sleeping soundly and selfishly while girls shaded his face from the sun, looking down at him with a love he accepted with a lordly indifference. What had once been the easiest thing in the world now felt entirely out of reach; Ruth’s lap seemed like an impossible destination. Yet his very hesitation was the secret strength of his pursuit. It was this restraint that kept her from feeling alarmed. Being fastidious and shy herself, she remained unaware of the perilous direction their connection was taking. She drifted toward him subtly and unconsciously, while he, sensing that growing proximity, yearned to be bold but remained paralyzed by fear.
source_text: Tongue-tied by inexperience and by excess of ardor, wooing unwittingly and awkwardly, Martin continued his approach by contact. The touch of his hand was pleasant to her, and something deliciously more than pleasant. Martin did not know it, but he did know that it was not distasteful to her. Not that they touched hands often, save at meeting and parting; but that in handling the bicycles, in strapping on the books of verse they carried into the hills, and in conning the pages of books side by side, there were opportunities for hand to stray against hand. And there were opportunities, too, for her hair to brush his cheek, and for shoulder to touch shoulder, as they leaned together over the beauty of the books. She smiled to herself at vagrant impulses which arose from nowhere and suggested that she rumple his hair; while he desired greatly, when they tired of reading, to rest his head in her lap and dream with closed eyes about the future that was to be theirs. On Sunday picnics at Shellmound Park and Schuetzen Park, in the past, he had rested his head on many laps, and, usually, he had slept soundly and selfishly while the girls shaded his face from the sun and looked down and loved him and wondered at his lordly carelessness of their love. To rest his head in a girl’s lap had been the easiest thing in the world until now, and now he found Ruth’s lap inaccessible and impossible. Yet it was right here, in his reticence, that the strength of his wooing lay. It was because of this reticence that he never alarmed her. Herself fastidious and timid, she never awakened to the perilous trend of their intercourse. Subtly and unaware she grew toward him and closer to him, while he, sensing the growing closeness, longed to dare but was afraid.

text: A sense of insanity took hold, yet she chose to ignore its implications. Her individual identity faded, replaced by a fundamental feminine yearning; even the gentlest pressure of her weight seemed to fulfill that deep-seated craving. Her exhaustion had vanished completely. Martin remained silent. Had he spoken, the enchantment would have been shattered, but his quiet, loving restraint allowed the magic to endure. He felt lightheaded and overwhelmed, unable to grasp the reality of the situation. The experience felt so sublime that it seemed like a fever dream. He fought back a wild impulse to abandon the ropes and the helm just to pull her into his embrace. Sensing that such an action would be a mistake, he felt relieved that the demands of the boat kept his hands busy, shielding him from the lure of his own impulses. Nevertheless, he handled the vessel with intentional clumsiness, letting the wind escape the sails without shame to stretch out their progress toward the northern bank. Reaching the shore would force him to change course, and their physical connection would be severed. He navigated with such expertise that he slowed their momentum without the wranglers noticing, silently absolving his past grueling journeys for having prepared him for this exquisite night. His hard-won command over the elements and the craft allowed him to guide the boat while she rested beside him, her precious weight leaning upon his shoulder.
source_text: It was a madness, but she refused to consider the madness. She was no longer herself but a woman, with a woman’s clinging need; and though she leaned ever so lightly, the need seemed satisfied. She was no longer tired. Martin did not speak. Had he, the spell would have been broken. But his reticence of love prolonged it. He was dazed and dizzy. He could not understand what was happening. It was too wonderful to be anything but a delirium. He conquered a mad desire to let go sheet and tiller and to clasp her in his arms. His intuition told him it was the wrong thing to do, and he was glad that sheet and tiller kept his hands occupied and fended off temptation. But he luffed the boat less delicately, spilling the wind shamelessly from the sail so as to prolong the tack to the north shore. The shore would compel him to go about, and the contact would be broken. He sailed with skill, stopping way on the boat without exciting the notice of the wranglers, and mentally forgiving his hardest voyages in that they had made this marvellous night possible, giving him mastery over sea and boat and wind so that he could sail with her beside him, her dear weight against him on his shoulder.

text: “So then—” She inhaled deeply, with the guileless air of a child, as she gave utterance to the furthest reaches of her ambition. “I desire a dairy farm—a prosperous dairy farm. Many cows, vast acreage, and rich, green grass. I would have it near San Lorenzo, for my sister resides there. I would sell my milk in the city of Oakland and amass a great fortune. Marco and Luca would not labor with the herd; they would attend their lessons. In time, they would become skilled engineers for the great railroad. Yes, that is the life I crave: the dairy farm.”
source_text: “Well, den—” She drew a big breath like a child, as she voiced to the uttermost all she cared to demand of life. “I lika da have one milka ranch—good milka ranch. Plenty cow, plenty land, plenty grass. I lika da have near San Le-an; my sister liva dere. I sella da milk in Oakland. I maka da plentee mon. Joe an’ Nick no runna da cow. Dey go-a to school. Bimeby maka da good engineer, worka da railroad. Yes, I lika da milka ranch.”

text: Once Teresa had taken the letter to the mailbox, Martin reclined and began to reflect. As it turned out, the reports were accurate. The *White Mouse* issued payment upon accepting a piece. "The Whirlpool" ran three thousand words. If he trimmed it by a third, two thousand words would remain. A forty-dollar payment for that length meant a rate of two cents per word. Payment upon acceptance at two cents a word—the press had been honest. To think he had dismissed the *White Mouse* as a low-tier publication! Clearly, he lacked a true understanding of the magazine market. He had considered the *Transcontinental* a top-tier journal, yet it only offered a penny for every ten words. He had written off the *White Mouse* as insignificant, yet it paid twenty times the rate of the *Transcontinental* and provided immediate payment upon acceptance.
source_text: The letter despatched to the letter-box by Teresa, Martin lay back and thought. It wasn’t a lie, after all. The _White Mouse_ paid on acceptance. There were three thousand words in “The Whirlpool.” Cut down a third, there would be two thousand. At forty dollars that would be two cents a word. Pay on acceptance and two cents a word—the newspapers had told the truth. And he had thought the _White Mouse_ a third-rater! It was evident that he did not know the magazines. He had deemed the _Transcontinental_ a first-rater, and it paid a cent for ten words. He had classed the _White Mouse_ as of no account, and it paid twenty times as much as the_ Transcontinental_ and also had paid on acceptance.

text: “No, it was for less than a week’s labor. Please hand me that gray-covered ledger on the far corner of the table.” He opened the book and began leafing through the pages at a brisk pace. “Yes, I was right. Four days for ‘The Ring of Bells’ and two for ‘The Whirlpool.’ That totals forty-five dollars for a week of work, or one hundred and eighty dollars a month. That exceeds any salary I could currently earn. Besides, I’m only just starting out. A thousand dollars a month isn't too much to aim for if I want to provide you with everything you deserve. Even a salary of five hundred a month would be insufficient. That forty-five dollars is merely the beginning. Just wait until I find my rhythm. Then you’ll see what I can really do.”
source_text: “No, for less than a week’s work. Please pass me that book over on the far corner of the table, the account book with the gray cover.” He opened it and began turning over the pages rapidly. “Yes, I was right. Four days for ‘The Ring of Bells,’ two days for ‘The Whirlpool.’ That’s forty-five dollars for a week’s work, one hundred and eighty dollars a month. That beats any salary I can command. And, besides, I’m just beginning. A thousand dollars a month is not too much to buy for you all I want you to have. A salary of five hundred a month would be too small. That forty-five dollars is just a starter. Wait till I get my stride. Then watch my smoke.”

text: I simply wasn't aware," he replied. "The possibility of lepers never entered my mind. After I abandoned the schooner and reached the shore, I struck out for the interior, looking for a place to hide. For three days, I sustained myself on wild guavas, *ohia*-apples, and bananas gathered from the jungle. On the fourth day, I stumbled upon a path—a simple foot-trail. It climbed steadily as it led further inland. This was the direction I intended to take, and the trail showed evidence of recent use. At one point, it followed the spine of a ridge that was as narrow as a knife's edge. The path atop the crest was less than three feet wide, with vertical cliffs on either side dropping hundreds of feet into the depths. A lone man, well-supplied with ammunition, could have defended that position against a hundred thousand.
source_text: “Because I didn’t know,” he answered. “I never dreamed of lepers. When I deserted the schooner and landed on the beach, I headed inland for some place of hiding. For three days I lived off guavas, _ohia_-apples, and bananas, all of which grew wild in the jungle. On the fourth day I found the trail—a mere foot-trail. It led inland, and it led up. It was the way I wanted to go, and it showed signs of recent travel. At one place it ran along the crest of a ridge that was no more than a knife-edge. The trail wasn’t three feet wide on the crest, and on either side the ridge fell away in precipices hundreds of feet deep. One man, with plenty of ammunition, could have held it against a hundred thousand.

text: The two men watched Mr. White with anticipation, but he only laughed and gave a shrug. His conscience, at least, was clear. He had initially joined the *Transcontinental* to study the craft of magazine writing, yet he had primarily received an education in finance instead. The publication owed him four months of back pay, and he understood that the printer’s bills had to be settled long before the associate editor’s salary.
source_text: Both men looked expectantly at Mr. White, but that gentleman laughed and shrugged his shoulders. His conscience was clean at any rate. He had come into the _Transcontinental_ to learn magazine-literature, instead of which he had principally learned finance. The _Transcontinental_ owed him four months’ salary, and he knew that the printer must be appeased before the associate editor.

text: “I concede the truth of your words. I was a captive to my own timidity. My affection was too narrow, but I have mastered a deeper devotion. I love you in your entirety: for your present self, your past, and the very crucible that forged you. I prize the qualities that set you apart from my own world; though your philosophies elude me now, I know I am capable of reaching them. I will make their study my life’s work. Even your vices—the smoke and the coarse tongue—are facets of your being that I now hold dear. I am still a student of life. This quarter-hour has taught me more than years past. My presence here, this bold step, is the first fruit of my education. Oh, Julian!”
source_text: “I know that much you have said is so. I have been afraid of life. I did not love you well enough. I have learned to love better. I love you for what you are, for what you were, for the ways even by which you have become. I love you for the ways wherein you differ from what you call my class, for your beliefs which I do not understand but which I know I can come to understand. I shall devote myself to understanding them. And even your smoking and your swearing—they are part of you and I will love you for them, too. I can still learn. In the last ten minutes I have learned much. That I have dared to come here is a token of what I have already learned. Oh, Martin!—”

text: Upon returning to his room, he lay down and drifted into a sleep as peaceful as an infant’s. The night’s events had left no mark on him, as his mind had become unresponsive to any external influence. The brief flicker of affection he felt for Joe had vanished almost instantly; by the next minute, he was merely annoyed by the former laundry worker’s presence and the exhausting demand for conversation. Even the fact that he was set to depart for his cherished South Seas in five days held no meaning for him. He shut his eyes and rested soundly for eight continuous hours. He did not stir, nor did he shift his body or experience any dreams. Sleep had transformed into total oblivion, and every morning he woke with a sense of sorrow. Existence was a source of weariness and anxiety, and the very passage of time was a nuisance.
source_text: Once in his room he got into bed and slipped off to sleep as gently as a child. The occurrences of the evening had made no impression on him. His mind was dead to impressions. The glow of warmth with which he met Joe had been most fleeting. The succeeding minute he had been bothered by the ex-laundryman’s presence and by the compulsion of conversation. That in five more days he sailed for his loved South Seas meant nothing to him. So he closed his eyes and slept normally and comfortably for eight uninterrupted hours. He was not restless. He did not change his position, nor did he dream. Sleep had become to him oblivion, and each day that he awoke, he awoke with regret. Life worried and bored him, and time was a vexation.

text: Existence struck him as a fierce, bleaching radiance that tormented the fragile sight of a convalescent. Through every hour of wakefulness, reality flared with a jagged brilliance that pressed upon his spirit. It was a searing, intolerable burden. This was Julian’s inaugural journey in the grand cabins; previously, his life at sea had been spent in the cramped forecastle or the suffocating, coal-blackened bowels of the hull. In those years, as he climbed the iron stairs from the furnace heat, he would catch fleeting sights of the elite in their crisp, pale garments, idling under canopies that shielded them from the elements. They were pampered by deferential attendants who catered to every trivial desire, and to Julian, their sphere of existence had seemed a literal heaven. Yet here he sat, the ship’s most distinguished guest, positioned at the captain’s right hand, while his heart wandered back to the soot and the steerage in a futile search for the paradise he had misplaced. He had failed to find a new sanctuary, and the old one was now beyond his reach.
source_text: Life was to him like strong, white light that hurts the tired eyes of a sick person. During every conscious moment life blazed in a raw glare around him and upon him. It hurt. It hurt intolerably. It was the first time in his life that Martin had travelled first class. On ships at sea he had always been in the forecastle, the steerage, or in the black depths of the coal-hold, passing coal. In those days, climbing up the iron ladders out the pit of stifling heat, he had often caught glimpses of the passengers, in cool white, doing nothing but enjoy themselves, under awnings spread to keep the sun and wind away from them, with subservient stewards taking care of their every want and whim, and it had seemed to him that the realm in which they moved and had their being was nothing else than paradise. Well, here he was, the great man on board, in the midmost centre of it, sitting at the captain’s right hand, and yet vainly harking back to forecastle and stoke-hole in quest of the Paradise he had lost. He had found no new one, and now he could not find the old one.

text: The primitive, unbidden drive for existence asserted itself. He stopped his rhythmic paddling, but as the tide crested over his lips, his limbs thrashed out in a sharp, upward surge. *The will to live*, he reflected, the notion eliciting a derisive scowl. Truly, he possessed a spirit of such magnitude that, through one ultimate act of defiance, it might extinguish itself and fall into silence.
source_text: It was the automatic instinct to live. He ceased swimming, but the moment he felt the water rising above his mouth the hands struck out sharply with a lifting movement. The will to live, was his thought, and the thought was accompanied by a sneer. Well, he had will,—ay, will strong enough that with one last exertion it could destroy itself and cease to be.

text: In the foyer below was a mail-slot that would accept no letter, and a push-button from which no mortal hand could coax a ring. Also belonging to the scene was a card bearing the name “Mr. Julian Beaumont Smith.” The “Beaumont” had been brandished with pride during a former season of prosperity when its owner was paid thirty-five dollars a week. Now, with the income shrunken to twenty, the letters of “Beaumont” looked faint, as though they were seriously considering a retreat into a modest and unassuming B. But whenever Mr. Julian Beaumont Smith arrived at his flat above, he was called “Jules” and greeted with a fervent hug by Mrs. Julian Beaumont Smith, who...
source_text: In the vestibule below was a letter-box into which no letter would go, and an electric button from which no mortal finger could coax a ring. Also appertaining thereunto was a card bearing the name “Mr. James Dillingham Young.” The “Dillingham” had been flung to the breeze during a former period of prosperity when its possessor was being paid $30 per week. Now, when the income was shrunk to $20, the letters of “Dillingham” looked blurred, as though they were thinking seriously of contracting to a modest and unassuming D. But whenever Mr. James Dillingham Young came home and reached his flat above he was called “Jim” and greatly hugged by Mrs. James Dillingham Young, already

text: Mr. Skidder was particularly notable, as he had mentally cast her as the leading lady in a secret, unexpressed romantic play of his own creation. Then there was Mr. Hoover, a forty-five-year-old who was as portly and wealthy as he was dim-witted. The youthful Mr. Evans was also among them, feigning a dry, hollow cough in hopes that she might urge him to quit smoking. While the men collectively regarded her as the most delightful and spirited woman imaginable, the judgmental huffs from the women on the stairs remained entirely unrelenting.
source_text: Especially Mr. Skidder, who had cast her in his mind for the star part in a private, romantic (unspoken) drama in real life. And especially Mr. Hoover, who was forty-five, fat, flush and foolish. And especially very young Mr. Evans, who set up a hollow cough to induce her to ask him to leave off cigarettes. The men voted her “the funniest and jolliest ever,” but the sniffs on the top step and the lower step were implacable.

text: “I was certain of it, Dempsey,” she remarked, her gaze turning lackluster even through her tears. “I was aware he was a Guinea; his actual name is Tony Spinelli. I rushed in as soon as I heard that you and he were brawling. Those Guineas are always armed with knives. But you don’t see the truth of it, Dempsey. I’ve never had a man of my own. I was tired of being the third wheel to Anna and Jimmy every night, so I convinced him to go by O’Sullivan and brought him here. I knew he wouldn’t stand a chance if he arrived as a Dago. I suppose I’ll be leaving the club for good now.”
source_text: “I knew it, Dempsey,” she said, as her eyes grew dull even in their tears. “I knew he was a Guinea. His name’s Tony Spinelli. I hurried in when they told me you and him was scrappin’. Them Guineas always carries knives. But you don’t understand, Dempsey. I never had a fellow in my life. I got tired of comin’ with Anna and Jimmy every night, so I fixed it with him to call himself O’Sullivan, and brought him along. I knew there’d be nothin’ doin’ for him if he came as a Dago. I guess I’ll resign from the club now.”

text: I entered the world as a yellow puppy, though the specifics of my birth date, location, lineage, and weight are entirely unknown. My very first memory involves an elderly woman at the intersection of Broadway and Twenty-third, who held me in a basket while trying to sell me to a stout lady. This "Old Mother Hubbard" was hyping me up for all she was worth, claiming I was a genuine Pomeranian-Hambletonian-Red-Irish-Cochin-China-Stoke-Pogis fox terrier. The heavy-set woman fumbled through the fabric scraps in her shopping bag until she finally cornered a five-dollar bill and handed it over. From that second on, I was a pampered pet—"mommy’s own precious little wootsey-squidlums." Tell me, dear reader, have you ever been lifted by a two-hundred-pound woman smelling of Camembert cheese and floral perfume while she nuzzles her nose all over you, chanting in a high-society soprano: “Oh, who’s a sweet little oodlum, doodlum, woodlum, toodlum, itsy-bitsy skoodlums?”
source_text: I was born a yellow pup; date, locality, pedigree and weight unknown. The first thing I can recollect, an old woman had me in a basket at Broadway and Twenty-third trying to sell me to a fat lady. Old Mother Hubbard was boosting me to beat the band as a genuine Pomeranian-Hambletonian-Red-Irish-Cochin-China-Stoke-Pogis fox terrier. The fat lady chased a V around among the samples of gros grain flannelette in her shopping bag till she cornered it, and gave up. From that moment I was a pet—a mamma’s own wootsey squidlums. Say, gentle reader, did you ever have a 200-pound woman breathing a flavour of Camembert cheese and Peau d’Espagne pick you up and wallop her nose all over you, remarking all the time in an Emma Eames tone of voice: “Oh, oo’s um oodlum, doodlum, woodlum, toodlum, bitsy-witsy skoodlums?”

text: You scruffy, woodchuck-hunting old mutt," I said to him. "You moon-howling, rabbit-tracking, egg-thieving beagle, don't you realize I have no desire to leave you? Can't you see we are both like those pups lost in the forest, while the mistress plays the part of the wicked uncle, chasing you with a kitchen towel and coming for me with flea ointment and a pink ribbon for my tail? Why don't we just give all that up and be partners for good?
source_text: “You old flea-headed woodchuck-chaser,” I said to him—“you moon-baying, rabbit-pointing, egg-stealing old beagle, can’t you see that I don’t want to leave you? Can’t you see that we’re both Pups in the Wood and the missis is the cruel uncle after you with the dish towel and me with the flea liniment and a pink bow to tie on my tail. Why not cut that all out and be pards forever more?”

text: My dear Arthur," Aunt Beatrice sighed, "I wish you would not dwell so upon finance. Opulence is a trifle against a sincere bond; love is invincible. Had he but voiced his intentions sooner!
