She could never have turned our Julian away. But I fear the hour has slipped by, leaving him no occasion to speak. All your hoarded treasures cannot purchase the contentment your son requires.
source_text: “Oh, Anthony,” sighed Aunt Ellen, “I wish you would not think so much of money. Wealth is nothing where a true affection is concerned. Love is all-powerful. If he only had spoken earlier! She could not have refused our Richard. But now I fear it is too late. He will have no opportunity to address her. All your gold cannot bring happiness to your son.”

text: Then the almanac turned deceitful, proclaiming the arrival of spring. But the season only appears when it is ready. The icy drifts of January remained as unyielding as stone throughout the side streets. Street organs continued to perform “In the Good Old Summertime” with their characteristic December spirit and flair. Men began signing thirty-day notes to purchase Easter finery, and janitors cut off the heat. When these circumstances arise, it is clear that the city remains firmly within winter’s grasp.
source_text: And then the almanac lied, and said that spring had come. Spring comes when it comes. The frozen snows of January still lay like adamant in the crosstown streets. The hand-organs still played “In the Good Old Summertime,” with their December vivacity and expression. Men began to make thirty-day notes to buy Easter dresses. Janitors shut off steam. And when these things happen one may know that the city is still in the clutches of winter.

text: A new truth dawned upon Maud’s reviving senses. Suddenly, she had risen from a hopeless, humble worshiper to a peer of the irresistible Beatrice. She was now a temptress herself, a mark for the Archer, a maiden who must show reserve while the victors feasted. Man had judged her waist attainable and her mouth delightful. The impulsive and amorous Mr. Higgins had, in effect, performed a miraculous feat of overnight renovation. He had taken the coarse burlap of her plainness, washed, dried, and refined it, returning it to her as delicate, patterned lace—the very gown of Aphrodite.
source_text: Another thing dawned upon Tildy’s recovering wits. In a moment she had advanced from a hopeless, lowly admirer to be an Eve-sister of the potent Aileen. She herself was now a man-charmer, a mark for Cupid, a Sabine who must be coy when the Romans were at their banquet boards. Man had found her waist achievable and her lips desirable. The sudden and amatory Seeders had, as it were, performed for her a miraculous piece of one-day laundry work. He had taken the sackcloth of her uncomeliness, had washed, dried, starched and ironed it, and returned it to her sheer embroidered lawn—the robe of Venus herself.

text: Such news piqued the traveler’s interest, prompting him to pose a series of inquiries regarding the path taken by the phantom—for so he designated the creature. Not long after, when we were at last in private, he remarked, “I have surely stirred your wonder, much as I have that of these worthy folk; yet you possess too much delicacy to press me for answers.”
source_text: This aroused the stranger’s attention, and he asked a multitude of questions concerning the route which the dæmon, as he called him, had pursued. Soon after, when he was alone with me, he said, “I have, doubtless, excited your curiosity, as well as that of these good people; but you are too considerate to make inquiries.”

text: In this manner, the unfortunate victim attempted to console both her companions and herself. She did indeed find the quiet acceptance she had sought. Yet I, the actual perpetrator, felt the undying worm of remorse alive within my breast, a sensation that permitted no hope or relief. Elizabeth also grieved and was heavy-hearted, but hers was the sorrow of the pure; it was like a cloud drifting across a luminous moon, momentarily veiling its brilliance without ever staining it. Agony and hopelessness had struck the very center of my heart; I carried an inner hell that nothing could ever douse. We remained with Justine for several hours, and it was only with the utmost struggle that Elizabeth could bring herself to leave. “I wish,” she cried out, “that I might perish with you, for I cannot bear to live in a world so full of wretchedness.”
source_text: Thus the poor sufferer tried to comfort others and herself. She indeed gained the resignation she desired. But I, the true murderer, felt the never-dying worm alive in my bosom, which allowed of no hope or consolation. Elizabeth also wept and was unhappy, but hers also was the misery of innocence, which, like a cloud that passes over the fair moon, for a while hides but cannot tarnish its brightness. Anguish and despair had penetrated into the core of my heart; I bore a hell within me which nothing could extinguish. We stayed several hours with Justine, and it was with great difficulty that Elizabeth could tear herself away. “I wish,” cried she, “that I were to die with you; I cannot live in this world of misery.”

text: Before long, a soft radiance crept across the firmament, instilling within me a sense of delight. I rose abruptly to witness a luminous orb ascending from the depths of the forest. I stared at the sight with a species of awe. Though its transit was gradual, it illuminated my way, allowing me to resume my quest for wild fruit. Still shivering, I happened upon a voluminous mantle beneath the boughs of a tree; I draped it about my person and reclined upon the earth. My intellect held no clear thoughts, for everything was muddled. I was conscious only of light, of the cravings of stomach and throat, and of the prevailing shadows; a multitude of noises echoed in my hearing, and diverse aromas assailed me from every quarter. The solitary feature I could truly discern was that brilliant satellite, and I anchored my vision upon it with contentment.
source_text: “Soon a gentle light stole over the heavens and gave me a sensation of pleasure. I started up and beheld a radiant form rise from among the trees. [The moon] I gazed with a kind of wonder. It moved slowly, but it enlightened my path, and I again went out in search of berries. I was still cold when under one of the trees I found a huge cloak, with which I covered myself, and sat down upon the ground. No distinct ideas occupied my mind; all was confused. I felt light, and hunger, and thirst, and darkness; innumerable sounds rang in my ears, and on all sides various scents saluted me; the only object that I could distinguish was the bright moon, and I fixed my eyes on that with pleasure.

text: It quickly became apparent that while the visitor spoke in distinct syllables and possessed a language of her own, she could not understand the dwellers, nor could they comprehend her. They made various gestures that remained obscure to me, yet I perceived that her arrival radiated a sudden light through the house, scattering their misery as the sun drives off the morning vapors. Julian seemed exceptionally joyous, welcoming his exotic guest with looks of pure enchantment. Seraphina, the soul of gentleness, pressed her lips to the hands of the lovely newcomer and, indicating her brother, made signs which I interpreted to mean that he had been steeped in melancholy until her arrival. A few hours passed in this manner, their faces reflecting a delight whose source I did not yet know. Soon, I noticed the frequent repetition of certain sounds which the guest echoed, signaling her attempt to learn their tongue; the thought instantly struck me that I should adopt these same instructions for my own benefit. The stranger acquired some twenty words in that first lesson; though many were sounds I had already mastered, I drew great profit from those that were unfamiliar.
source_text: “I soon perceived that although the stranger uttered articulate sounds and appeared to have a language of her own, she was neither understood by nor herself understood the cottagers. They made many signs which I did not comprehend, but I saw that her presence diffused gladness through the cottage, dispelling their sorrow as the sun dissipates the morning mists. Felix seemed peculiarly happy and with smiles of delight welcomed his Arabian. Agatha, the ever-gentle Agatha, kissed the hands of the lovely stranger, and pointing to her brother, made signs which appeared to me to mean that he had been sorrowful until she came. Some hours passed thus, while they, by their countenances, expressed joy, the cause of which I did not comprehend. Presently I found, by the frequent recurrence of some sound which the stranger repeated after them, that she was endeavouring to learn their language; and the idea instantly occurred to me that I should make use of the same instructions to the same end. The stranger learned about twenty words at the first lesson; most of them, indeed, were those which I had before understood, but I profited by the others.

text: A violent delirium followed in the wake of these horrors. For two months, I lingered at the very threshold of the grave; my frantic utterances, I was later informed, were ghastly to endure, as I proclaimed myself the slayer of Arthur, of Elena, and of Sterling. At times, I beseeched my caretakers to help me extinguish the demon that haunted my existence; at others, I felt the phantom hands of the beast constricting my throat, and I howled in a convulsion of agony and fright. It was a singular mercy that I spoke in my mother tongue, for only Magistrate Thorne grasped the meaning of my words; yet my wild thrashing and desolate cries were more than enough to petrify the other onlookers.
source_text: A fever succeeded to this. I lay for two months on the point of death; my ravings, as I afterwards heard, were frightful; I called myself the murderer of William, of Justine, and of Clerval. Sometimes I entreated my attendants to assist me in the destruction of the fiend by whom I was tormented; and at others I felt the fingers of the monster already grasping my neck, and screamed aloud with agony and terror. Fortunately, as I spoke my native language, Mr. Kirwin alone understood me; but my gestures and bitter cries were sufficient to affright the other witnesses.

text: In my youth," he remarked, "I felt certain that I was meant for a monumental undertaking. While my emotions run deep, I once held a clarity of mind that made me capable of extraordinary feats. This sense of my own value sustained me where others might have faltered; I considered it a sin to squander in idle sorrow the abilities that could benefit humanity. Considering my finished labor—the animation of a sentient and reasoning being—I could not view myself as merely another ordinary dreamer. Yet that very pride, which bolstered me at the start, now only casts me deeper into despair. My ambitions and dreams have vanished, and much like the celestial being who craved absolute power, I am now bound in a perpetual torment. I possessed a fertile imagination alongside a rigorous capacity for logic and execution; through the marriage of these traits, I envisioned and then brought a man to life. Even today, I cannot recall the visions I had during that unfinished labor without a surge of emotion. My mind soared through the heavens, at times rejoicing in my own strength and at others consumed by the potential impact of my discovery. Since childhood, I was filled with grand expectations and a towering drive, yet look at how far I have fallen. Oh, my friend, had you encountered me in my former self, you would find me unrecognizable in this wretched condition. Melancholy seldom touched me; I felt carried forward by a grand fate, until I collapsed, never to stand again.
source_text: “When younger,” said he, “I believed myself destined for some great enterprise. My feelings are profound, but I possessed a coolness of judgment that fitted me for illustrious achievements. This sentiment of the worth of my nature supported me when others would have been oppressed, for I deemed it criminal to throw away in useless grief those talents that might be useful to my fellow creatures. When I reflected on the work I had completed, no less a one than the creation of a sensitive and rational animal, I could not rank myself with the herd of common projectors. But this thought, which supported me in the commencement of my career, now serves only to plunge me lower in the dust. All my speculations and hopes are as nothing, and like the archangel who aspired to omnipotence, I am chained in an eternal hell. My imagination was vivid, yet my powers of analysis and application were intense; by the union of these qualities I conceived the idea and executed the creation of a man. Even now I cannot recollect without passion my reveries while the work was incomplete. I trod heaven in my thoughts, now exulting in my powers, now burning with the idea of their effects. From my infancy I was imbued with high hopes and a lofty ambition; but how am I sunk! Oh! My friend, if you had known me as I once was, you would not recognise me in this state of degradation. Despondency rarely visited my heart; a high destiny seemed to bear me on, until I fell, never, never again to rise.”

text: An admirable vigor was manifest in nearly every expression; indeed, some possessed a certain sublimity and tenderness that shamed one's trivial censures into stillness. A most exalted benevolence and untainted virtue rested upon the features of the knight titled Sir Galahad, and similarly upon those of the sovereign; furthermore, a sense of imperial splendor and distinction was evident in the massive form and stately demeanor of Sir Launcelot of the Lake.
source_text: There was a fine manliness observable in almost every face; and in some a certain loftiness and sweetness that rebuked your belittling criticisms and stilled them. A most noble benignity and purity reposed in the countenance of him they called Sir Galahad, and likewise in the king's also; and there was majesty and greatness in the giant frame and high bearing of Sir Launcelot of the Lake.

text: I watched the lad with a sense of pity, noting the veil of profound sadness that stole over his expression. Glancing where he looked, I saw a venerable man with a long white beard, garbed in a loose black habit, who had risen and now stood by the table on precarious legs. His ancient head swayed with a frail motion as his dim, drifting eyes roved over the gathering. The same look of wretchedness I had seen in the young servant was visible on every face in the room—the silent endurance of brute animals who recognize that they must suffer without complaint.
source_text: I looked at the boy in sorrow; and as I looked I saw the cloud of a deep despondency settle upon his countenance. I followed the direction of his eye, and saw that a very old and white-bearded man, clothed in a flowing black gown, had risen and was standing at the table upon unsteady legs, and feebly swaying his ancient head and surveying the company with his watery and wandering eye. The same suffering look that was in the page's face was observable in all the faces around--the look of dumb creatures who know that they must endure and make no moan.

text: Immediately, we were immersed in the wilderness. The dawn of autumn brought a refreshing chill to these timbered seclusions, making the early hours exceptionally graceful. From the heights, we gazed down upon lush emerald basins where rivers coiled through the landscape, interspersed with tufts of woodland and grand, solitary oaks that threw stark, dark silhouettes across the ground. Beyond these vales, mountain tiers draped in azure vapor receded in rolling waves toward the horizon; here and there, a faint smudge of ash or pearl atop a distant crest signaled the site of a stronghold. We traversed expansive, dew-laden commons with the silence of phantoms, our strides muffled by the springy sod. We drifted through clearings illuminated by a verdant glow—a hue born of the sun-soaked ceiling of boughs—while beside us, a freezing, translucent creek skipped and babbled over its rocky bed, producing a gentle, rhythmic sighing that was pleasant to the ear. At intervals, we turned from the light to penetrate the hallowed, opulent shadows of the deep woods. In that dimness, wary beasts flickered past, disappearing into the thicket before one could even fix a gaze upon the source of the rustle. Only the most industrious birds were awake, occupied with a melody in one quarter or a spat in another, while the muffled, distant thrumming of a woodpecker echoed from some unreachable sanctuary within the forest’s core. Then, at last, we would step back out into the dazzling brightness.
source_text: Straight off, we were in the country. It was most lovely and pleasant in those sylvan solitudes in the early cool morning in the first freshness of autumn. From hilltops we saw fair green valleys lying spread out below, with streams winding through them, and island groves of trees here and there, and huge lonely oaks scattered about and casting black blots of shade; and beyond the valleys we saw the ranges of hills, blue with haze, stretching away in billowy perspective to the horizon, with at wide intervals a dim fleck of white or gray on a wave-summit, which we knew was a castle. We crossed broad natural lawns sparkling with dew, and we moved like spirits, the cushioned turf giving out no sound of footfall; we dreamed along through glades in a mist of green light that got its tint from the sun-drenched roof of leaves overhead, and by our feet the clearest and coldest of runlets went frisking and gossiping over its reefs and making a sort of whispering music, comfortable to hear; and at times we left the world behind and entered into the solemn great deeps and rich gloom of the forest, where furtive wild things whisked and scurried by and were gone before you could even get your eye on the place where the noise was; and where only the earliest birds were turning out and getting to business with a song here and a quarrel yonder and a mysterious far-off hammering and drumming for worms on a tree trunk away somewhere in the impenetrable remotenesses of the woods. And by and by out we would swing again into the glare.

text: Presently Sir Valerius wheeled his mount and bore down upon Alaric with his lance. Observing the charge, Sir Alaric braced his shield, and both knights couched their spears, colliding with the full fury of their galloping steeds; each warrior struck the other so violently in the center of his buckler, yet it was Alaric’s shaft that splintered.
source_text: “Then Sir Marhaus turned his horse and rode toward Gawaine with his spear. And when Sir Gawaine saw that, he dressed his shield, and they aventred their spears, and they came together with all the might of their horses, that either knight smote other so hard in the midst of their shields, but Sir Gawaine's spear brake--”

text: “Oh, pray, attend to your devoted maiden! Could one endowed with my modest arts truly address the vanquisher of Malakor in such a fashion without intending a mere drollery? Through my divinations, I had already envisioned your arrival, and by their light, I recognized you the very instant you entered this hall. I did but perform this small charade in the hope of provoking some manifestation of your skill, never doubting that you would strike the guards down with mystical fires, reducing them to heaps of ash—a wonder far exceeding my own capabilities, yet one I have long felt a childish eagerness to behold.”
source_text: “La, but do list to thine handmaid! as if one gifted with powers like to mine might say the thing which I have said unto one who has vanquished Merlin, and not be jesting. By mine enchantments I foresaw your coming, and by them I knew you when you entered here. I did but play this little jest with hope to surprise you into some display of your art, as not doubting you would blast the guards with occult fires, consuming them to ashes on the spot, a marvel much beyond mine own ability, yet one which I have long been childishly curious to see.”

text: We lingered for a duration in the dense shadow and silence, watching a ruby smudge on the horizon and straining to interpret a distant hum that ebbed and flowed fitfully through the night. At times it swelled, seeming for a moment more proximate; yet, when we anticipated it might reveal its source, it grew dull and subsided, its secret still guarded. We set off down the slope toward it, the winding track casting us at once into a nearly palpable darkness—an obscurity packed tight between two high ramparts of timber. We groped forward for perhaps half a mile, the murmur growing increasingly audible. The impending storm became more threatening, bringing occasional tremors of wind, pale flickers of lightning, and the low growls of remote thunder. I was at the front. I collided with an object—a soft, heavy mass that gave way slightly under my momentum; in that same instant, the lightning blazed, and a foot from my face hung the distorted visage of a man dangling from a branch! It seemed to twitch, though it was actually still. It was a morbid sight. Immediately, an ear-splitting crash of thunder followed, and the very floor of heaven gave way; the rain fell in a deluge. Regardless, we had to attempt to cut him down, for there was a chance that a spark of life remained, was there not? The lightning became rapid and piercing, the woods alternating between the clarity of midday and the void of midnight. One heartbeat the man was suspended before me in a fierce light, and the next he was vanished into the gloom. I told the Sovereign we must lower him. The Sovereign at once demurred.
source_text: We stood there awhile, in the thick darkness and stillness, looking toward the red blur in the distance, and trying to make out the meaning of a far-away murmur that rose and fell fitfully on the night. Sometimes it swelled up and for a moment seemed less remote; but when we were hopefully expecting it to betray its cause and nature, it dulled and sank again, carrying its mystery with it. We started down the hill in its direction, and the winding road plunged us at once into almost solid darkness--darkness that was packed and crammed in between two tall forest walls. We groped along down for half a mile, perhaps, that murmur growing more and more distinct all the time. The coming storm threatening more and more, with now and then a little shiver of wind, a faint show of lightning, and dull grumblings of distant thunder. I was in the lead. I ran against something--a soft heavy something which gave, slightly, to the impulse of my weight; at the same moment the lightning glared out, and within a foot of my face was the writhing face of a man who was hanging from the limb of a tree! That is, it seemed to be writhing, but it was not. It was a grewsome sight. Straightway there was an ear-splitting explosion of thunder, and the bottom of heaven fell out; the rain poured down in a deluge. No matter, we must try to cut this man down, on the chance that there might be life in him yet, mustn't we? The lightning came quick and sharp now, and the place was alternately noonday and midnight. One moment the man would be hanging before me in an intense light, and the next he was blotted out again in the darkness. I told the king we must cut him down. The king at once objected.

text: He was happy to comply. He filled them, and I carried them away. I didn't dare reveal that the miller-gun was a personal invention of mine, nor that I had issued a formal decree requiring every merchant in the realm to stock them and sell them at a state-mandated price. This was a mere pittance, and the shopkeeper kept the profit rather than the government. We supplied the items at no cost.
source_text: He would, with pleasure. He filled them, and I took them with me. I couldn't venture to tell him that the miller-gun was a little invention of my own, and that I had officially ordered that every shopkeeper in the kingdom keep them on hand and sell them at government price--which was the merest trifle, and the shopkeeper got that, not the government. We furnished them for nothing.

text:
Clarence supported my revolutionary goals, though with a particular caveat. He favored a republic that abolished social castes but retained a hereditary monarchy at its helm instead of an elected leader. He argued that any people who had experienced the delight of idolizing a royal family could never be deprived of it without withering away from profound sadness. When I pointed out that kings were a hazard, he suggested substituting them with cats. He was certain a feline dynasty would serve every necessary function. They would be just as effective as any human royals, possess equal intellect, and display the same blend of virtues and vices, including a tendency to pick fights with other royal cats. They would be hilariously conceited and absurd without ever realizing it, and they would cost absolutely nothing. Furthermore, their claim to divine right would be as legitimate as any other royal line; "Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV, King by the Grace of God," would sound just as dignified as the same title applied to a typical human monarch in his finery. 

"Moreover," he noted in his polished, modern tongue, "the temperament of these cats would generally be far superior to that of the average king. This would provide a tremendous moral boost to the public, as a nation always patterns its ethics after its ruler. Since the veneration of royalty is based on emotion rather than logic, these elegant and harmless animals would easily become as hallowed as any other sovereign—perhaps even more so. People would eventually observe that they never hang, behead, or imprison anyone, nor do they perpetrate any cruelties or injustices. Thus, they would be entitled to a deeper level of affection and respect than a standard human king, and they would surely receive it. The gaze of a troubled world would soon settle upon this kind and gentle model, causing royal despots to vanish. Their subjects would fill those empty thrones with kittens from our own royal stock; we would become a sort of factory, exporting monarchs to every nation. Within forty years, all of Europe would be under feline rule, and we would be the ones supplying the cats. An age of eternal global peace would then begin... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow—fzt!—wow!"
source_text: Clarence was with me as concerned the revolution, but in a modified way. His idea was a republic, without privileged orders, but with a hereditary royal family at the head of it instead of an elective chief magistrate. He believed that no nation that had ever known the joy of worshiping a royal family could ever be robbed of it and not fade away and die of melancholy. I urged that kings were dangerous. He said, then have cats. He was sure that a royal family of cats would answer every purpose. They would be as useful as any other royal family, they would know as much, they would have the same virtues and the same treacheries, the same disposition to get up shindies with other royal cats, they would be laughably vain and absurd and never know it, they would be wholly inexpensive; finally, they would have as sound a divine right as any other royal house, and “Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV by the grace of God King,” would sound as well as it would when applied to the ordinary royal tomcat with tights on. “And as a rule,” said he, in his neat modern English, “the character of these cats would be considerably above the character of the average king, and this would be an immense moral advantage to the nation, for the reason that a nation always models its morals after its monarch's. The worship of royalty being founded in unreason, these graceful and harmless cats would easily become as sacred as any other royalties, and indeed more so, because it would presently be noticed that they hanged nobody, beheaded nobody, imprisoned nobody, inflicted no cruelties or injustices of any sort, and so must be worthy of a deeper love and reverence than the customary human king, and would certainly get it. The eyes of the whole harried world would soon be fixed upon this humane and gentle system, and royal butchers would presently begin to disappear; their subjects would fill the vacancies with catlings from our own royal house; we should become a factory; we should supply the thrones of the world; within forty years all Europe would be governed by cats, and we should furnish the cats. The reign of universal peace would begin then, to end no more forever.... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow--fzt!--wow!”

text: Mr. Direck represented a familiar American archetype. He bore a striking resemblance to those wholesome, well-groomed figures found in the pages of American periodicals—the cheerful men who grin while endorsing "Fizgig" products, or insist that "Wilkins" is the superior choice, or boast that their "Chesson" shirts never wrinkle. At this moment, however, his steady smile accompanied a different refrain: "Excellent. It’s English." He took immense delight in everything that deviated from the American norm, celebrating every detail he deemed authentically British. During the rail journey to London, the sight of the Cheshire hills divided into a patchwork of tiny fields had moved him to joyful laughter, and the discovery of a train carriage lacking a corridor provoked a satisfied chuckle. After a brief hesitation over the propriety of the gesture, he had bestowed a lavish tip upon the courteous, friendly guard. Later, he wandered through his London hotel in a state of near-rapture, exclaiming "Lordy! Lordy! My *word!*" as he confirmed the charming lack of telephones, central heating, or private bathrooms. During breakfast, a waiter who appeared to have stepped directly from the pages of Dickens feigned ignorance at the mention of "cereals" and served his egg in a porcelain cup identical to those depicted in *Punch* cartoons. When he finally ventured out to view the Thames, it seemed almost surreal in its diminutiveness; it was the most modest river he had ever encountered. He found himself fighting the urge to adopt a heavy drawl and stop a stranger to ask, "Say! Is this narrow, damp trench truly the legendary River Thames?"
source_text: Mr. Direck was a type of man not uncommon in America. He was very much after the fashion of that clean and pleasant-looking person one sees in the advertisements in American magazines, that agreeable person who smiles and says, "Good, it's the Fizgig Brand," or "Yes, it's a Wilkins, and that's the Best," or "My shirt-front never rucks; it's a Chesson." But now he was saying, still with the same firm smile, "Good. It's English." He was pleased by every unlikeness to things American, by every item he could hail as characteristic; in the train to London he had laughed aloud with pleasure at the chequer-board of little fields upon the hills of Cheshire, he had chuckled to find himself in a compartment without a corridor; he had tipped the polite yet kindly guard magnificently, after doubting for a moment whether he ought to tip him at all, and he had gone about his hotel in London saying "Lordy! Lordy! My _word!_" in a kind of ecstasy, verifying the delightful absence of telephone, of steam-heat, of any dependent bathroom. At breakfast the waiter (out of Dickens it seemed) had refused to know what "cereals" were, and had given him his egg in a china egg-cup such as you see in the pictures in _Punch_. The Thames, when he sallied out to see it, had been too good to be true, the smallest thing in rivers he had ever seen, and he had had to restrain himself from affecting a marked accent and accosting some passer-by with the question, "Say! But is this little wet ditch here the Historical River Thames?"

text:
To Mr. Sterling, the hamlet appeared like a canvas by Morland. A tavern projected its painted emblem, the Pembroke Crest, over the thoroughfare; beside it stood a stone basin—the sort where a Dickensian coachman might douse a hypocrite—and a jade-colored table sat expectantly by the portal. A village store and several delightful dwellings, each adorned with the Ashbourne insignia, framed the common, where a flock of geese marched in military precision. 

Passing beneath a lych-gate, Mr. Thorne guided his companion into the graveyard, where they discovered crumbling, lichen-clad markers, including a seventeenth-century stone carved with a grim death’s-head. Within the sanctuary, three massive funerary hatchments hung aloft, and a lateral chapel honored the Ashbournes and the Lords of Sedgewick. Its monuments ranged from colorful Tudor carvings to a cavernous stained-glass window of the most tawdry Victorian production. They also noted Gothic brasses of former rectors and a marble knight resting beside his dame, relics of a vanished dynasty that had held sway over Willow’s Reach long before the Ashbournes’ tenure. 

Upon exiting the porch, the pair encountered the ideal clergyman, Mr. Merryweather—a stout, jovial soul with a booming, inclusive laugh and a voice that seemed to wrap around them. "Exploring the motherland, eh?" he remarked to Mr. Sterling. "How splendid of you Americans to visit! Quite splendid..."
source_text: The village reminded Mr. Direck of Abbey's pictures. There was an inn with a sign standing out in the road, a painted sign of the Clavering Arms; it had a water trough (such as Mr. Weller senior ducked the dissenter in) and a green painted table outside its inviting door. There were also a general shop and a number of very pleasant cottages, each marked with the Mainstay crest. All this was grouped about a green with real geese drilling thereon. Mr. Britling conducted his visitor (through a lych gate) into the church-yard, and there they found mossy, tumble-down tombstones, one with a skull and cross-bones upon it, that went back to the later seventeenth century. In the aisle of the church were three huge hatchments, and there was a side chapel devoted to the Mainstay family and the Barons Homartyn, with a series of monuments that began with painted Tudor effigies and came down to a vast stained glass window of the vilest commercial Victorian. There were also mediæval brasses of parish priests, and a marble crusader and his lady of some extinguished family which had ruled Matching's Easy before the Mainstays came. And as the two gentlemen emerged from the church they ran against the perfect vicar, Mr. Dimple, ample and genial, with an embracing laugh and an enveloping voice. "Come to see the old country," he said to Mr. Direck. "So Good of you Americans to do that! So Good of you...."

text: Yet, that detonation did not disturb or inconvenience Mr. Britling, Mr. Manning, or Mr. Direck in the least. Because it occurred far beyond the horizon of this spherical earth, it was impossible to hear or see from Matching’s Easy. Nevertheless, the blast was incredibly loud. It took place in an open area beside a river winding through a dense Oriental city—a place bristling with white minarets and surrounded by barren hills under a scorching afternoon sun. The sound came from a black package that Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria, with remarkable composure, had just hurled from his open-topped car, where it had landed moments earlier after being thrown from the quay. It exploded upon hitting the cobblestones directly beneath the front of the second vehicle in the motorcade, shattering the car’s front end and wounding both the aide-de-camp inside and several onlookers. The assailant was immediately seized by the crowd. The procession halted. A massive upheaval erupted among the vibrantly dressed throng, a feverish intensity that stood in sharp contrast to the Sunday tranquility of Matching’s Easy.
source_text: But neither Mr. Britling nor Mr. Manning nor Mr. Direck was interrupted or incommoded in the slightest degree by that report. Because it was too far off over the curve of this round world to be either heard or seen at Matching's Easy. Nevertheless it was a very loud report. It occurred at an open space by a river that ran through a cramped Oriental city, a city spiked with white minarets and girt about by bare hills under a blazing afternoon sky. It came from a black parcel that the Archduke Francis Ferdinand of Austria, with great presence of mind, had just flung out from the open hood of his automobile, where, tossed from the side of the quay, it had descended a few seconds before. It exploded as it touched the cobbled road just under the front of the second vehicle in the procession, and it blew to pieces the front of the automobile and injured the aide-de-camp who was in it and several of the spectators. Its thrower was immediately gripped by the bystanders. The procession stopped. There was a tremendous commotion amongst that brightly-costumed crowd, a hot excitement in vivid contrast to the Sabbath calm of Matching's Easy....

text: At this point, Mr. Thorne was starting to grasp the fundamental maneuvers of the game. He was also recognizing that Beatrice was the most agile, swift, and tireless participant on the pitch. He served as her scout and distributor, developing a silent rapport with her. Any impulse to shield her had vanished completely. Facing them, Arthur and the girl from the sidecar, bolstered by Sterling, put up a formidable fight. Arthur was as lithe as a leopard. A "Four-Three" result seemed imminent, but then Arthur, the lanky Sikh, and Mrs. Arthur leveled the score. They nearly achieved the same feat to win, but Mr. Fletcher saved the day with a massive diagonal strike that found Mr. Thorne. He sprinted toward Sterling, feinted, and centered the ball to Beatrice. A frantic skirmish broke out on the left; Sterling knocked the ball out of bounds, and a young Pemberton threw it back in; the front line fumbled, but the cushioned matron recovered it. Onward once more! This final push will surely be the one!
source_text: By this time Mr. Direck was beginning to master the simple strategy of the sport. He was also beginning to master the fact that Cecily was the quickest, nimblest, most indefatigable player on the field. He scouted for her and passed to her. He developed tacit understandings with her. Ideas of protecting her had gone to the four winds of Heaven. Against them Teddy and a sidecar girl with Raeburn in support made a memorable struggle. Teddy was as quick as a cat. "Four-Three" looked like winning, but then Teddy and the tall Indian and Mrs. Teddy pulled square. They almost repeated this feat and won, but Mr. Manning saved the situation with an immense oblique hit that sent the ball to Mr. Direck. He ran with the ball up to Raeburn and then dodged and passed to Cecily. There was a lively struggle to the left; the ball was hit out by Mr. Raeburn and thrown in by a young Britling; lost by the forwards and rescued by the padded lady. Forward again! This time will do it!

text: The encounter with Edith occurred in a manner that shared no similarities with his memories of the late Mary. He met her through conventional social circles, whereas Mary had been a student at Newnham while he was a fellow at Pembroke; their discovery of one another had been a stroke of luck, characterized by chance and a touch of secrecy. That relationship had been marked by excitement and a certain impulsive energy. With Edith, however, he observed her, made a choice, and was required to court her. There was no sudden collision of souls, but rather a process of formal request and eventual agreement. Edith held a science degree from London University along with other similar credentials, and she viewed the world from beneath a wide brow and undulating brown hair with steady, observant eyes that seemed to hide nothing at all. This transparency was so unfathomable to Mr. Britling that he fell in love and married her primarily because of the tranquil mystery she seemed to possess. For a period after their wedding, he navigated those brown depths, intensely attempting to measure their true scale.
source_text: He met Edith under circumstances that did not in any way recall his lost Mary. He met her, as people say, "socially"; Mary, on the other hand, had been a girl at Newnham while he was a fellow of Pembroke, and there had been something of accident and something of furtiveness in their lucky discovery of each other. There had been a flush in it; there was dash in it. But Edith he saw and chose and had to woo. There was no rushing together; there was solicitation and assent. Edith was a Bachelor of Science of London University and several things like that, and she looked upon the universe under her broad forehead and broad-waving brown hair with quiet watchful eyes that had nothing whatever to hide, a thing so incredible to Mr. Britling that he had loved and married her very largely for the serenity of her mystery. And for a time after their marriage he sailed over those brown depths plumbing furiously.

text: Do you see that fallen timber over there? I rested upon it only the day before yesterday, my mind filled with thoughts of you. Won't you accompany me to that spot and sit a while? From that seat, one is granted a view—oh, a most charming English prospect!—offering a glimpse of the manor, those stands of trees, and the valley stretching toward the lily pond. I should so like to include you in my remembrance of it.
source_text: "You see that old felled tree there. I was sitting on it the day before yesterday and thinking of you. Will you come there and sit with me on it? When you sit on it you get a view, oh! a perfectly lovely English view, just a bit of the house and those clumps of trees and the valley away there with the lily pond. I'd love to have you in my memory of it...."

text: Britain had not yet officially entered the conflict, but the heavens themselves seemed to be aligning toward that inevitable conclusion. It was as though a catastrophe of such immense proportions required a gradual onset. Mr. Britling harbored no uncertainty, even as a full-page advertisement in the *Daily News*—set in massive font by an anonymous sponsor—desperately urged the nation not to support "Russia the Terrible," the traditional bogeyman of emotional Radicals. The prevailing reports were broad and dramatic, though often lacking in accuracy. Claims circulated that German troops had invaded Belgium and the Netherlands, and that British ships were being impounded in the Kiel Canal. With a moratorium now in effect, the emerging stories of food hoarding proved that Mrs. Faber was just one of many highly-strung individuals reacting to the crisis.
source_text: England was not yet at war, but all the stars were marching to that end. It was as if an event so vast must needs take its time to happen. No doubt was left upon Mr. Britling's mind, though a whole-page advertisement in the _Daily News_, in enormous type and of mysterious origin, implored Great Britain not to play into the hands of Russia, Russia the Terrible, that bugbear of the sentimental Radicals. The news was wide and sweeping, and rather inaccurate. The Germans were said to be in Belgium and Holland, and they had seized English ships in the Kiel Canal. A moratorium had been proclaimed, and the reports of a food panic showed Mrs. Faber to be merely one example of a large class of excitable people.

text: As long as it could only be addressed in fragments," Mr. Britling remarked. "And that is exactly where the immense potential of this conflict lies. Now, everything has become malleable. We have the chance to reshape the entire globe. Just a week ago, we were embroiled in bitter arguments over matters far too insignificant for human restlessness. Suddenly, we find ourselves at the dawn of a new era. This is a historic turning point. The world is now like clay, waiting for humanity to shape it as they see fit. This marks both the conclusion and the birth of an age. It is an event far more profound than the Reformation or the French Revolution... and we are living through it.
source_text: "So long as it could only be dealt with piecemeal," said Mr. Britling. "And that is just where the tremendous opportunity of this war comes in. Now everything becomes fluid. We can redraw the map of the world. A week ago we were all quarrelling bitterly about things too little for human impatience. Now suddenly we face an epoch. This is an epoch. The world is plastic for men to do what they will with it. This is the end and the beginning of an age. This is something far greater than the French Revolution or the Reformation.... And we live in it...."

text: This inclination remained evident as the afternoon progressed, surfacing in both offhand comments and intentional jokes. The younger boys had encountered the goose step, and the discovery filled them with a sense of wonder and amusement. They found it nearly impossible to believe that anyone would agree to move in such a preposterous manner. After experimenting with the gait themselves, they began a crusade to spread the practice. Letty and Cissie, who had stopped by the Dower House for tea and updates, were soon recruited to join Teddy and Hugh. The group of six moved across the lawn in exaggerated, scissor-like strides, laughing and swaying as they went. "Left," Hugh called out. "Left."
source_text: And this disposition kept coming to the surface throughout the afternoon, now in a casual allusion, now in some deliberate jest. The small boys had discovered the goose step, and it filled their little souls with amazement and delight. That human beings should consent to those ridiculous paces seemed to them almost incredibly funny. They tried it themselves, and then set out upon a goose-step propaganda. Letty and Cissie had come up to the Dower House for tea and news, and they were enrolled with Teddy and Hugh. The six of them, chuckling and swaying, marched, in vast scissor strides across the lawn. "Left," cried Hugh. "Left."

text: It seems this event should have heralded its arrival with a clarion blast. Yet, until this moment, it has merely been a distant clamor, a disturbance noted as if from an adjacent residence. Now, the kingdom gradually stirs. The metropolis resembles a confused dreamer emerging from heavy rest to find conflagration and threat, upheaval and frantic appeals for assistance, right at the doorstep. The avenues convey precisely this mood. Citizens are watchful and attentive. One senses that at any second, during a hush or a lull, there might arrive from the far horizon, drifting over the chimney pots, the dim resonance of cannons or the faint, pained wails of small French or Belgian townships in their hour of torment.
source_text: "One feels the thing ought to have come upon us like the sound of a trumpet. Instead, until now, it has been like a great noise, that we just listened to, in the next house.... And now slowly the nation awakes. London is just like a dazed sleeper waking up out of a deep sleep to fire and danger, tumult and cries for help, near at hand. The streets give you exactly that effect. People are looking about and listening. One feels that at any moment, in a pause, in a silence, there may come, from far away, over the houses, faint and little, the boom of guns or the small outcries of little French or Belgian villages in agony...."

text: Melodies and moonbeams blended with an earnest German sensibility and robust singing. There was a grand picnic in a Stuhl Wagen, and the boys played disciplined circle games with the German children, while Hugh shared long walks and private thoughts with Kurt and Karl. The festivities reached a peak with a spirited dance, accompanied by Romani musicians found by Mr. Britling. During the revelry, the Germans demonstrated amusing contests involving baskets, potatoes, and forfeits, while the English introduced their hosts to the free-spirited two-step. The company sang "Britannia, Rule the Waves" and "Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles" in unison. Adam Meyer climbed onto a chair to deliver a grand oration in a dialect more pronounced than ever, as everyone enjoyed beer and syrups in the moonlight beneath the linden tree.
source_text: Music, moonlight, the simple German sentiment, the hearty German voices, the great picnic in a Stuhl Wagen, the orderly round games the boys played with the German children, and the tramps and confidences Hugh had with Kurt and Karl, and at last a crowning jollification, a dance, with some gipsy musicians whom Mr. Britling discovered, when the Germans taught the English various entertaining sports with baskets and potatoes and forfeits and the English introduced the Germans to the licence of the two-step. And everybody sang "Britannia, Rule the Waves," and "Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles," and Adam Meyer got on a chair and made a tremendous speech more in dialect than ever, and there was much drinking of beer and sirops in the moonlight under the linden....

text: As I promised, I will describe the trenches. The essential thing to understand is their narrowness; they are a kind of inverted wall, resembling massive fissures in the earth more than anything else. Perhaps I should begin by explaining how we reached them. We set out around one in the morning, weighed down by every piece of equipment a soldier could possibly carry. In addition, I lugged a kettle filled with water, while most of the men had bundles of firewood and some carried extra bread. We marched from our quarters along the road for a mile or more before cutting across the fields. Eventually, we reached a ridge and descended into a labyrinth of zigzagging trenches that led toward the front line. These trenches are deeper than a man’s height, so you can see nothing; it is like walking through a corridor of mud. You simply trudge along in single file. Every so often, someone stumbles into a rainwater drainage pit, curses at a patch of soft ground, or bumps into the man ahead of him. This seems to go on for hours. It certainly lasted an hour, so I expect we covered two or three miles. At one point, we crossed a hollow and a ditch where the trench was reinforced with sandbags and spanned by a plank. Stars were visible overhead, and occasionally, the flares they launch would illuminate the trench edges, providing a brief glimpse of a distant treetop or a factory roof. For a while afterward, it was more difficult to proceed because the glare left us blinded. Then, just as you began to believe this slog would never end, we reached the support trenches behind the firing line and found the men we were relieving ready to depart.
source_text: "And now about those trenches--as I promised. The great thing to grasp is that they are narrow. They are a sort of negative wall. They are more like giant cracks in the ground than anything else.... But perhaps I had better begin by telling how we got there. We started about one in the morning ladened up with everything you can possibly imagine on a soldier, and in addition I had a kettle--filled with water--most of the chaps had bundles of firewood, and some had extra bread. We marched out of our quarters along the road for a mile or more, and then we took the fields, and presently came to a crest and dropped into a sort of maze of zigzag trenches going up to the front trench. These trenches, you know, are much deeper than one's height; you don't see anything. It's like walking along a mud-walled passage. You just trudge along them in single file. Every now and then some one stumbles into a soakaway for rainwater or swears at a soft place, or somebody blunders into the man in front of him. This seems to go on for hours and hours. It certainly went on for an hour; so I suppose we did two or three miles of it. At one place we crossed a dip in the ground and a ditch, and the trench was built up with sandbags up to the ditch and there was a plank. Overhead there were stars, and now and then a sort of blaze thing they send up lit up the edges of the trench and gave one a glimpse of a treetop or a factory roof far away. Then for a time it was more difficult to go on because you were blinded. Suddenly just when you were believing that this sort of trudge was going on forever, we were in the support trenches behind the firing line, and found the men we were relieving ready to come back.

text: On three occasions this year," Raeburn remarked, "triumph has eluded us because of the sheer incompetence of our administrative staff. There is little point in seeking out individual culprits; the issue is one of national character. It stems from the fact that the graduates of our elite schools have never been taught to be punctual, to arrive at work precisely on time, to organize their own gear, or to perform any task with speed and precision—at least, nothing they can manage to delegate to others. One cannot expect individuals who are naturally relaxed to maintain a state of rigorous efficiency for long. Their entire upbringing and heritage work against it. They have a profound aversion to being perceived as overly fastidious; an Englishman would prefer to be a complete failure than to be labeled a prig. This is the reason we have forfeited three battles that should have been ours—along with countless lives, vast supplies, and time of immeasurable value. A full year has been squandered, and the morale of our nation has been broken.
source_text: "Three times this year," said Raeburn, "we have missed victories because of the badness of our staff work. It's no good picking out scapegoats. It's a question of national habit. It's because the sort of man we turn out from our public schools has never learnt how to catch trains, get to an office on the minute, pack a knapsack properly, or do anything smartly and quickly--anything whatever that he can possibly get done for him. You can't expect men who are habitually easy-going to keep bucked up to a high pitch of efficiency for any length of time. All their training is against it. All their tradition. They hate being prigs. An Englishman will be any sort of stupid failure rather than appear a prig. That's why we've lost three good fights that we ought to have won--and thousands and thousands of men--and material and time, precious beyond reckoning. We've lost a year. We've dashed the spirit of our people."

text: I am forged of cold iron, Martha. My spirit is my mother’s legacy, far removed from my father’s mildness. Julian is my entire world; he is the sole repository of whatever softness I once held. If that light is snuffed out, then let the darkness come. I have no intention of whimpering through my remaining days like those wretched, mourning creatures who crawl across the earth. If they have taken my man, I shall take their lives. It will be a reckoning of blood for blood and ruin for ruin. I shall hunt down the very instigators of this carnage, drawing close enough to feel their breath, and I will put an end to them and their lineage.
source_text: "I'm hard stuff, Cissie. I take after mother more than father. Teddy is my darling. All the tenderness of my life is Teddy. If it goes, it goes.... I won't crawl about the world like all these other snivelling widows. If they've killed my man I shall kill. Blood for blood and loss for loss. I shall get just as close to the particular Germans who made this war as I can, and I shall kill them and theirs....

text: His getaway followed shortly thereafter. It had taken remarkably little time for him to break free. He had been confined within a Belgian railway station, held in a waiting area alongside several French captives, when Allied aircraft launched an assault on the junction. The explosion claimed the lives of their sentry and two of the other detainees. Amidst the ensuing turmoil, the remaining men managed to flee into the nearby streets. Wagons filled with hay were ablaze, and a nearby fuel depot was on the verge of igniting. "In such circumstances, flight was the only option. To be discovered roaming the area would have meant certain execution."
source_text: Then afterwards he had escaped. In quite a little time he had escaped. He had been in a railway station somewhere in Belgium; locked in a waiting-room with three or four French prisoners, and the junction had been bombed by French and British aeroplanes. Their guard and two of the prisoners had been killed. In the confusion the others had got away into the town. There were trucks of hay on fire, and a store of petrol was in danger. "After that one was bound to escape. One would have been shot if one had been found wandering about."
